topics.title: Custodial Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT803 - ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOUR AND BUSINESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Custodial Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Keith Davis Framework)**
> The **Custodial Model** is one of the four historical models of Organizational Behaviour (alongside the Autocratic, Supportive, and Collegial models). It is a **security-oriented, resource-based approach** to managing people that emerged from the economic interpretation of the Hawthorne Studies (1924–1932). The model is built on the premise that **economic rewards and tangible benefits** (pensions, insurance, paid leave, stock options) are the primary drivers of long-term employee loyalty. Unlike the Autocratic Model (where loyalty is to the boss), the Custodial Model shifts the employee's psychological dependence from the boss to the **organization itself**, because the organization (not the individual supervisor) provides the long-term security.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Govt. Job Pension" Analogy**
> Imagine a **government bank employee** working in 1985. He earns a modest salary compared to a private tech startup, but he knows that after 30 years, he will receive a **lifetime pension, free medical care for his family, a provident fund, and a guaranteed retirement home**.
>
> - He does not love his **direct manager** (who may be rude or autocratic).
> - He does, however, **fear leaving the organization**, because no other employer will offer him a similar pension and security package.
> - His loyalty is to the **institution** (the bank), not the person.
>
> This is the **Custodial Model** in a single sentence: *"The organization acts as a father-figure/custodian, providing economic security in exchange for passive, obedient membership."*

### 1.3 Historical Roots and Origin

| Feature | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Originator** | Keith Davis (inherited from earlier theorists like **Oliver Sheldon**) |
| **Intellectual Root** | Hawthorne Studies (Elton Mayo) – employees responded to economic/social context, not just physical work conditions |
| **Period of Dominance** | 1930s – 1950s (Mass-production era, large unions, welfare capitalism) |
| **Motivational Anchor** | **Lower-level needs** of Maslow's Hierarchy – Physiological and Safety needs |
| **Operative Currency** | **Money & Economic Resources** (cash + fringe benefits + long-term security) |

### 1.4 KTU Key Term Glossary (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Terms for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> - **Custodian** → The organization itself (acts as parent figure)
> - **Dependent** → The employee (psychologically tied to the org)
> - **Custodialism** → The state of being economically secure but motivationally passive
> - **Ego-needs** (referenced in Custodial context) → Lower-level / security needs (NOT upper-level ego from Maslow's peak)
> - **Passive Cooperation** → The typical output of a custodial employee – does not oppose, but does not innovate either

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 The Seven Pillars of the Custodial Model (Keith Davis)

Every KTU OB question on this topic expects students to know the **seven diagnostic dimensions** that define an OB model. Here is the complete Custodial Model mapped against these seven pillars:

1. **Basis of OB Model** → Economic resources.
   *Why:* Because management realized workers respond to wages, benefits, and security more than to orders (Hawthorne finding).

2. **Managerial Orientation** → Money and fringe benefits (welfare-oriented manager).
   *Why:* The manager is trained to be a **benefits administrator** rather than a driver or facilitator.

3. **Employee Orientation** → Security and benefits.
   *Why:* Employees are motivated by what they *receive*, not what they *produce*.

4. **Psychological Result for Employee** → Dependence on the organization.
   *Why:* The pension plan is held by the company, the insurance is corporate, the housing loan is from the employer — leaving means losing all.

5. **Performance Result** → Passive cooperation.
   *Why:* Employees obey rules, but show low innovation, low initiative, and low engagement.

6. **Climate** → Security-oriented / Stable but rigid.
   *Why:* The workplace feels like a "safe fortress" — comfortable but stale.

7. **Needs Sought to Be Satisfied** → Security / Lower-level needs (Physiological + Safety needs of Maslow).

### 2.2 Analytical Comparison Table — The Four Models of OB (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Hot-Spot:** This comparative table is **THE MOST LIKELY 7-MARK QUESTION** in Module 1. Memorize the *Managerial Orientation* and *Psychological Result* rows column by column.

| Dimension | **Autocratic Model** | **Custodial Model** | **Supportive Model** | **Collegial Model** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Power | Economic Resources | Leadership | Partnership |
| **Managerial Orientation** | Authority | Money & Benefits | Support | Teamwork |
| **Employee Orientation** | Obedience | Security & Benefits | Job Performance | Responsible Behaviour |
| **Employee Psychological Result** | Dependence on Boss | Dependence on Organization | Participation | Self-discipline |
| **Performance Result** | Minimum performance | Passive cooperation | Awakened drives | Moderate enthusiasm |
| **Needs Satisfied** | Subsistence | Security | Status & Recognition | Self-actualization |
| **Era** | Pre-1900s | 1930s–1950s | 1950s–1970s | 1970s–Present |
| **Application Today** | Bureaucratic Govt. offices | PSU Banks, Old Public Sector Units | R&D Labs, IT firms | Modern Agile startups |

### 2.3 Why "Custodial" is *Not* "Supportive" — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students confuse *Custodial* with *Supportive* because both sound "caring." They are **diametrically opposite** in motivation philosophy.

$$
\text{Custodial} = \text{Satisfies LOWER-level needs} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Supportive} = \text{Satisfies UPPER-level needs}
$$

- The **Custodial** model asks: *"How do I keep the employee from leaving?"* (Retention through economic lock-in).
- The **Supportive** model asks: *"How do I make the employee want to perform?"* (Motivation through engagement and leadership).

### 2.4 Maslow Mapping Diagram (Conceptual)

The Custodial Model operates **only** on the bottom two layers of Maslow's pyramid:

$$
\text{Maslow}_{\text{Custodial}} = \{\text{Physiological Needs}\} \cup \{\text{Safety Needs}\}
$$

The three upper layers (Belonging, Esteem, Self-Actualization) are **deliberately ignored**, which is the model's greatest weakness — it produces security but **kills intrinsic motivation**.

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Industry Sector | Custodial Model Manifestation |
|---|---|
| **Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs)** in India (e.g., BSNL, Air India pre-privatization) | Lifetime employment + pension + free housing |
| **Government Banking (SBI pre-1990s)** | Provident Fund, Gratuity, Defined-Benefit Pension |
| **Japanese Keiretsu (e.g., Toyota pre-1990s)** | Lifetime employment + company housing + corporate welfare |
| **European Heavy Industry (Post-WWII)** | Strong union-driven welfare state inside factories |
| **Legacy Manufacturing in Kerala (Travancore Cements, FACT)** | Wage boards, subsidized canteens, housing colonies |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Conceptual Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Logical Derivation of the Custodial Model (Why Did It Arise?)

This section presents the **causal reasoning chain** that produced the Custodial Model. KTU examiners often ask this as a 7-mark essay question: *"Explain the evolution of the Custodial Model."*

> **Step 1 — The Autocratic Crisis (Pre-1924)**
> Industrial workplaces ran on the Autocratic Model (Bureaucratic, scientific management / Taylorism). Result: extremely high physical output, but equally high turnover, absenteeism, and labor unrest.

> **Step 2 — The Hawthorne Discovery (1924–1932)**
> Elton Mayo's experiments at Western Electric's Hawthorne Works revealed that workers were **not** purely economic machines. Social factors, group dynamics, and management's personal attention influenced output far more than lighting or wage rates alone.

> **Step 3 — The Economic Interpretation**
> Theorists like Keith Davis, Oliver Sheldon, and Mary Parker Follett (partially) argued: *"If workers want security above all, why not sell them security?"* This shifted the currency of motivation from **commands** to **economic resources**.

> **Step 4 — The Welfare Capitalism Era (1930s)**
> Companies like Kodak, IBM, and Sears introduced company towns, pensions, profit-sharing, and on-site healthcare. The Custodial Model was formalized as: *"Buy the worker's future, and you own their present."*

> **Step 5 — The Dependency Outcome**
> Once the worker's pension, insurance, and seniority rights are tied to one employer, leaving becomes financially irrational. This creates **organization-dependence**, the model's defining psychological fingerprint.

### 3.2 The Custodial "Equation" — A Conceptual Model

While there is no algebraic formula, we can express the Custodial Model's logic mathematically:

$$
\text{Loyalty to Organization} \; = \; f\left(E_{\text{benefits}} + T_{\text{tenure}}, \quad S_{\text{exit cost}}\right)
$$

Where:

- $E_{\text{benefits}}$ = Cumulative economic benefits (pension, gratuity, insurance)
- $T_{\text{tenure}}$ = Length of service (vesting multiplier)
- $S_{\text{exit cost}}$ = Financial penalty of leaving

> **Interpretation:** As $S_{\text{exit cost}} \uparrow$, organizational loyalty $\uparrow$ but **innovation** $\downarrow$.

This explains why PSUs have stable headcount but rarely disrupt markets — **they are custodially locked**.

### 3.3 The Three Critical Limitations (Analytical Matrix)

| # | Limitation | KTU Exam Keyword | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Disregards Higher-Order Needs** | "Lower-level needs only" | Employee feels secure but unfulfilled |
| 2 | **Promotes Passivity** | "Passive cooperation" | Low initiative, no innovation, slow growth |
| 3 | **Costly & Unsustainable** | "Economic burden" | High overhead; fails during recessions (e.g., 1991 Indian PSU crisis) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Conclusion of Custodial Derivation:**
> The Custodial Model represents a **half-way house** between the Autocratic Model (too coercive) and the Supportive Model (too idealistic for its era). It solved the *retention* problem of industry but created the *engagement* problem of the modern workplace, which later models had to solve.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: The Custodial Dependency Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Employee Performs Work] --> B{Organization Provides Economic Benefits}
    B --> C[Pension, Insurance, Gratuity, Housing]
    C --> D[Employee Experiences Security]
    D --> E[Psychological Dependence on Organization Forms]
    E --> F[Employee Avoids Leaving the Firm]
    F --> G[Continued Service & Loyalty]
    G --> A
    G --> H[Side Effect: Passive Cooperation and Low Innovation]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Note the **feedback loop** from G back to A — this is why custodial systems are **self-reinforcing** (lock-in effect). The side-effect branch (G → H) represents the model's primary weakness.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture: Custodial Model Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BASE[Foundation Layer]
        HAW[Hawthorne Studies Insights]
        MAS[Maslow Lower Needs]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Core Mechanism Layer]
        RES[Economic Resources as Currency]
        BEN[Benefit Administration]
    end

    subgraph BEHAV[Behavioral Output Layer]
        DEP[Dependence on Organization]
        PASS[Passive Cooperation]
        SEC[Security-Oriented Climate]
    end

    HAW --> RES
    MAS --> RES
    RES --> BEN
    BEN --> DEP
    DEP --> PASS
    BEN --> SEC
```

### 4.3 Position of Custodial Model in Maslow's Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M5[Self Actualization]:::top
    M4[Esteem Needs]:::top
    M3[Belonging and Love]:::top
    M2[Safety Needs]:::cust
    M1[Physiological Needs]:::cust

    classDef top fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef cust fill:#4a90e2,stroke:#003366,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px
```

> **Reading the pyramid:** The blue-highlighted layers (**Physiological** and **Safety**) are the **exclusive operating zone** of the Custodial Model. The three grey layers above are **NOT addressed** by this model.

### 4.4 Comparison Block: Custodial vs. Autocratic vs. Supportive (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CUSTODIAL[THE CUSTODIAL MODEL]
        C1[Basis: Economic Resources]
        C2[Manager: Money/Benefits Focused]
        C3[Employee: Security Oriented]
        C4[Result: Passive Cooperation]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    end

    subgraph AUTOCRATIC[THE AUTOCRATIC MODEL]
        A1[Basis: Power]
        A2[Manager: Authority Oriented]
        A3[Employee: Obedient]
        A4[Result: Minimum Performance]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    end

    subgraph SUPPORTIVE[THE SUPPORTIVE MODEL]
        S1[Basis: Leadership]
        S2[Manager: Support Oriented]
        S3[Employee: Performance Driven]
        S4[Result: Awakened Drives]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Custodial Model of organizational behaviour. Mention its managerial orientation.*

**Model Answer (Memorize This):**
> The Custodial Model of organizational behaviour is a security-based approach in which the organization provides economic and welfare benefits (such as pensions, insurance, and job security) to employees in exchange for their loyalty. The managerial orientation of this model is **money and benefits** — managers are essentially administrators of the welfare system rather than leaders or facilitators.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Identify the type of employee psychological result produced by the Custodial Model.*

**Model Answer:**
> The Custodial Model produces **dependence on the organization**. Employees become psychologically tied to the employer because their long-term economic security (pensions, benefits, seniority) is held within the firm, making them reluctant to leave despite possibly lacking motivation to perform at higher levels.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfall:** When answering 14-mark questions, students often write only about the **definition** (3 marks) and skip the **seven pillars** and **limitations**. This caps your score at 3–4 out of 14. You **MUST** cover: (1) Definition, (2) Managerial + Employee Orientation, (3) Psychological + Performance Result, (4) Need satisfied, (5) At least 2 real-world examples, (6) At least 2 limitations, (7) Comparison with at least one other model.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**"Explain the Custodial Model of Organizational Behaviour in detail. Compare it with the Autocratic Model."** [14 Marks]

##### Part (a) — Explain the Custodial Model in detail. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Custodial Model is one of the four classical models of OB proposed by Keith Davis. It is built on the principle that employees are motivated primarily by **economic security and welfare benefits**, and that long-term loyalty can be purchased through long-term rewards.

**Step 2 — Basis and Managerial Orientation (1 Mark):**
- **Basis of the model:** Economic resources.
- **Managerial orientation:** Money and benefits (welfare-focused manager).

**Step 3 — Employee Orientation and Result (2 Marks):**
- **Employee orientation:** Security and benefits.
- **Psychological result:** Dependence on the **organization** (not the boss).
- **Performance result:** **Passive cooperation** — employees comply but do not innovate.

**Step 4 — Needs Satisfied (1 Mark):**
The model satisfies the **lower-level needs** of Maslow's hierarchy — physiological (wages) and safety needs (pension, job security).

**Step 5 — Real-World Examples (1 Mark):**
- Government banks like SBI before 1990s.
- Public Sector Undertakings like BSNL, Air India (pre-privatization).
- Japanese keiretsu firms offering lifetime employment.

**Step 6 — Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Promotes **passivity** and kills innovation.
- Costly to maintain; fails during economic downturns.
- Ignores higher-level needs (esteem, belonging, self-actualization).

##### Part (b) — Compare with the Autocratic Model. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Comparison (KTU Format):**

| Parameter | Autocratic Model | Custodial Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Power | Economic Resources |
| **Managerial Orientation** | Authority | Money & Benefits |
| **Employee Orientation** | Obedience | Security & Benefits |
| **Dependence** | On the **Boss** | On the **Organization** |
| **Performance Result** | Minimum performance | Passive cooperation |
| **Needs Satisfied** | Subsistence (basic) | Security (slightly higher than subsistence) |
| **Era** | Pre-1900s | 1930s–1950s |
| **Example** | Pre-Ford assembly line | Old SBI / PSU Banks |

**Concluding Statement (1 Mark):**
The Custodial Model evolved as a direct response to the harshness of the Autocratic Model. Where the Autocratic Model produces fear of the *boss*, the Custodial Model produces dependence on the *organization*. However, both models fail to motivate employees intrinsically — only the Supportive and Collegial models that followed successfully addressed higher-order needs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**"Discuss the seven pillars of the Custodial Model. Why is this model considered unsuitable for modern IT companies?"** [14 Marks]

##### Part (a) — Explain the seven pillars of the Custodial Model. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The seven pillars are the standard diagnostic dimensions used by Keith Davis to describe any OB model:

1. **Basis of the Model → Economic Resources** (1 Mark)
   The model rests on the premise that people work for economic sustenance and security.

2. **Managerial Orientation → Money and Benefits** (1 Mark)
   Managers are oriented toward administering welfare schemes — gratuity, PF, medical aid, housing.

3. **Employee Orientation → Security and Benefits** (1 Mark)
   Employees join and stay for the security package, not for self-development.

4. **Psychological Result → Dependence on Organization** (1 Mark)
   Employees cannot afford to leave because benefits are non-portable.

5. **Performance Result → Passive Cooperation** (1 Mark)
   Employees obey but do not innovate.

6. **Organizational Climate → Security-oriented, rigid** (1 Mark)
   Stable but stagnant.

7. **Needs Satisfied → Physiological and Safety Needs** (1 Mark)
   Lower-level needs of Maslow.

##### Part (b) — Why is the Custodial Model unsuitable for modern IT companies? [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Critical Analysis:**

1. **Knowledge Workers ≠ Security Seekers** (2 Marks)
   Modern IT employees (engineers, designers, data scientists) seek challenging projects, autonomy, and growth — not pensions. The Custodial Model ignores these upper-level needs, leading to high attrition among millennials and Gen Z.

2. **Dynamic Industry Demands Agility** (1 Mark)
   IT firms need rapid pivots, continuous learning, and innovation. The Custodial Model's "passive cooperation" kills the very initiative firms like Infosys, TCS, Google, and Amazon depend on.

3. **No Portability of Skills** (1 Mark)
   IT professionals have globally portable skills. They do not want to be locked into one company for a pension — they would rather invest in mutual funds and ESOPs.

4. **Cost Burden is Unsustainable** (1 Mark)
   Custodial benefits (defined-benefit pensions, lifetime medical) are enormously expensive; modern firms prefer defined-contribution plans (PF, NPS, ESOP).

5. **Cultural Mismatch** (1 Mark)
   IT culture values collaboration, experimentation, and flat hierarchies — directly opposed to the custodial parent–child dynamic.

6. **Conclusion (1 Mark):**
   The modern IT sector thrives on the **Supportive and Collegial Models**, where leadership, teamwork, and self-discipline replace economic lock-in. Custodial elements (good health insurance, fair compensation) remain useful, but cannot be the *dominant* model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Never confuse **Custodial** (organization-dependence) with **Autocratic** (boss-dependence) — this alone loses 3 marks.
> 2. Always mention **Maslow's lower-level needs** explicitly when discussing Custodial; examiners give a free mark for this linkage.
> 3. Do **not** describe Custodial as "good" or "bad" — describe it as **"suitable for its era but inadequate for the modern knowledge economy."** This nuanced phrasing scores higher in KTU's answer-key matrix.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ The Custodial Model's **managerial orientation** is **money and benefits**, not authority or support.
- ✅ The model creates **dependence on the organization**, not on the boss.
- ✅ Its **performance result** is **passive cooperation** — not high performance and not minimum performance.
- ✅ It satisfies **Physiological and Safety needs** of Maslow's hierarchy (lower-level only).
- ✅ Its **basis** is **economic resources**, derived from the Hawthorne Studies.
- ✅ Its **organizational climate** is **security-oriented** but rigid and stagnant.
- ✅ It dominated from the **1930s to the 1950s**, during the welfare-capitalism era.
- ✅ Its **prime weakness** is that it ignores higher-order needs (esteem, self-actualization).
- ✅ Classic examples: **PSU banks (SBI pre-1990s), BSNL, Japanese lifetime-employment firms**.
- ✅ It is **NOT suitable for modern IT / knowledge-work industries** — these require the Supportive or Collegial models.
- ✅ It **differs from the Autocratic Model** in the locus of dependency (organization vs. boss) and the motivational currency (money vs. orders).
- ✅ Keith Davis formally placed the Custodial Model **between** Autocratic and Supportive in the evolutionary sequence of OB models.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supportive Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT803 - ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOUR AND BUSINESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supportive Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Supportive Model** (also known as **Likert's Management System 4** or **Participative Group Management**) is a leadership and organizational behaviour framework developed by **Rensis Likert** as part of his pioneering research on management systems at the University of Michigan's Institute for Social Research. The Supportive Model is built on the principle that an organization's performance is directly proportional to the quality of human resources and the degree to which managerial practices cultivate **participative, trust-based, and group-oriented work relationships**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the Supportive Model falls under the foundational theories of leadership and organizational behaviour, and is characterized by:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *The Supportive Model is a participative management system in which managers adopt a supportive, team-oriented, and people-centred supervisory style. It emphasizes mutual trust, open communication, overlapping group structures, joint decision-making, and the economic and ego-fulfillment of subordinates. It is regarded as the most effective and humanistic of Likert's four management systems.*

---

### 1.2 The Four Likert Management Systems (Contextual Framework)

To understand where the Supportive Model fits, you must first understand Likert's classification of organizational systems:

| System No. | Management System | Core Philosophy |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **System 1** | **Exploitative Authoritative** | Autocratic, top-down, fear-driven |
| **System 2** | **Benevolent Authoritative** | Paternalistic, condescending master-servant relationship |
| **System 3** | **Consultative** | Managers consult subordinates but retain final decision authority |
| **System 4** | **Supportive (Participative Group)** | Fully participative, trust-based, group decision-making |

The **Supportive Model is System 4**, and is universally regarded as Likert's ideal management system for achieving both high productivity and high employee satisfaction.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Family-Run Restaurant**
>
> Imagine a family-run restaurant where the head chef (the manager) treats every kitchen assistant (the subordinate) as a **trusted member of the family**:
>
> - The chef doesn't bark orders; he **asks for opinions** on the menu ("What do you think about adding a new dessert?").
> - The assistants feel safe enough to **speak up if a recipe is failing** — there's no fear of punishment.
> - The team **collectively decides** on the festival menu through a quick morning huddle.
> - The chef focuses on **empowering the team**, removing obstacles (low stock, broken equipment), and creating a positive work atmosphere.
> - Everyone feels proud of the restaurant's success because they **own the outcome**.
>
> This is exactly what the Supportive Model looks like inside an organization. The manager's job shifts from "commanding" to "supporting" — like a coach rather than a dictator. The result? **Better dishes (productivity)** AND **happier chefs (job satisfaction)**.

---

### 1.4 Core Foundational Pillars of the Supportive Model

The Supportive Model rests on **three foundational pillars** that distinguish it from all other management systems:

1. **Supportive Relationships** — Managers act as supportive resources, not controllers.
2. **Group Decision-Making & Supervision** — Decisions flow from overlapping work groups, not from individual authority.
3. **High Performance Goals** — Ambitious yet achievable goals are set jointly, with continuous improvement as a cultural norm.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exam:** Think of the Supportive Model as the **"3T Formula"** — **T**rust, **T**eamwork, and **T**ransparent communication. If a KTU question asks "List the three principles of the Supportive Model," this 3T mnemonic will help you recall the framework instantly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Supportive Model

The Supportive Model is built on multiple interlocking variables. Likert identified these as **causal, intervening, and end-result variables** that operate together to determine organizational effectiveness. The model must be studied as a system of **mutual, overlapping influence**, not as a list of isolated traits.

#### A. Leadership Processes in the Supportive Model

- Managers exhibit **sensitivity to subordinates' needs** and view people as **valuable human assets**.
- Leadership is **democratic and participative**, with a strong emphasis on **coaching and mentoring**.
- The supervisor's primary function is to **remove obstacles** that hinder subordinates' performance.
- The manager behaves like a **facilitator**, not an authority figure.

#### B. Motivational Forces

- The model applies **Maslow's higher-order needs** (Esteem, Self-Actualization) and **Herzberg's Motivator-Hygiene theory**.
- Subordinates are motivated by **participation, achievement, recognition, and growth** — not by fear or economic incentive alone.
- The focus is on **ego-satisfaction and self-fulfillment** through meaningful work.

#### C. Communication Processes

- Communication flows in **all directions** — upward, downward, and laterally (horizontal and diagonal).
- Information is **accurate, timely, and free-flowing**.
- Subordinates feel psychologically safe to **challenge ideas upward** without fear of retaliation.

#### D. Interaction-Influence Processes

- Influence is **broadly distributed** across all levels of the organization.
- Subordinates have **substantial influence** over decisions, goals, and methods.
- The relationship between superiors and subordinates is characterized by **mutual trust and respect**.

#### E. Decision-Making Processes

- Decisions are made **through group processes** in **overlapping work groups**.
- Each employee belongs to **multiple groups**, with group memberships partially shared between hierarchical levels — this is the famous **"linking pin" concept**.
- The supervisor is the **link between the superior's group and the subordinate's group**, ensuring organizational alignment.

#### F. Goal-Setting or Controlling Processes

- Goals are **set jointly** through negotiation between managers and subordinates.
- **High performance goals** are the norm — challenging but achievable.
- Control is **decentralized**, with each unit monitoring its own performance against agreed goals.

#### G. Performance Outcomes (End Results)

- The model aims to achieve **both high productivity and high employee satisfaction** — a key philosophical departure from older theories that assumed a trade-off.
- Outcomes include: better quality, lower absenteeism, lower turnover, stronger commitment, and higher morale.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a high-yield revision resource. Memorize it thoroughly for KTU Part A (3-mark) short-answer questions.**

| Sl. No. | Variable | Feature in Supportive Model | Contrast with System 1 (Exploitative) |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Leadership Style** | Democratic, supportive, coaching | Autocratic, dictatorial |
| 2 | **Motivation** | Higher-order needs, participation | Fear, threats, economic rewards |
| 3 | **Communication** | Free flow in all directions | Downward only, distorted |
| 4 | **Decision-Making** | Group-based, linking pin structure | Top-down, unilateral |
| 5 | **Trust Level** | Complete trust in subordinates | No trust, suspicion |
| 6 | **Goal-Setting** | Jointly set, high performance | Orders issued from above |
| 7 | **Control Mechanism** | Self-control, decentralized | Centralized policing |
| 8 | **Conflict Resolution** | Open discussion, win-win | Suppressed or punitive |
| 9 | **Supervisor's Role** | Facilitator, supporter | Overseer, controller |
| 10 | **Subordinate's Role** | Active participant, empowered | Passive order-taker |

---

### 2.3 The "Linking Pin" Concept (Likert's Most Cited Idea)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Likert):**
> *A "linking pin" is a person who holds membership in two or more overlapping groups within the organization. Every supervisor is simultaneously a subordinate in a higher-level group and a leader in a lower-level group. This overlap is the mechanism through which decisions, values, and information propagate vertically and laterally through the organization.*

**Why is the Linking Pin essential to the Supportive Model?**
- It ensures **seamless coordination** between hierarchical levels.
- It transforms the rigid chain-of-command into an **interlocking web of influence**.
- It is the **mechanism of group decision-making** that distinguishes System 4 from all other systems.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Supportive Model in Engineering & Project Management

| Engineering Field | Application of Supportive Model |
|:---|:---|
| **Software Development** | Agile & Scrum teams, self-organizing squads, daily stand-ups |
| **Civil Engineering** | Cross-functional project teams, joint risk-assessment workshops |
| **Mechanical Industry** | Kaizen groups, Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) teams |
| **IT Service Management** | DevOps teams, peer-review culture, blameless post-mortems |
| **R&D Labs** | Scientist-led innovation clusters, participative research direction |

The Supportive Model is the philosophical foundation of **modern flat organizations, matrix structures, and knowledge-economy teams**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Conceptual Derivation

### 3.1 Deriving the Supportive Model from Likert's Research

Likert's Supportive Model was not a theoretical abstraction — it was the **empirical conclusion** of decades of rigorous research at the University of Michigan. The derivation proceeds as follows:

---

**Step 1: Define the Dependent Variable (What determines organizational success?)**

Likert hypothesized that organizational success depends on three classes of variables:

$$
\text{Organizational Effectiveness} = f(\text{Causal Variables}, \text{Intervening Variables}, \text{End-Result Variables})
$$

Where:
- **Causal Variables** are the structural and leadership elements an organization can deliberately alter (leadership, policy, structure, training, etc.).
- **Intervening Variables** are internal states (motivation, attitudes, perception, trust) that result from causal variables.
- **End-Result Variables** are the ultimate outcomes (productivity, profit, cost, turnover, quality).

---

**Step 2: Establish the Research Premise**

Likert studied high-performing versus low-performing organizations in industries such as insurance, banking, manufacturing, and government. He found a **strong correlation** between the management system employed and the organization's performance.

---

**Step 3: Extract the Common Features of High-Performing Organizations**

After analysing hundreds of organizations, Likert extracted seven commonalities among the best-performing units. These seven features **define the Supportive Model**:

$$
\text{Supportive Model} = \{\text{Trust} \cap \text{Participation} \cap \text{Group Decisions} \cap \text{Open Communication} \cap \text{Linking Pin} \cap \text{Joint Goal-Setting} \cap \text{High Performance Goals}\}
$$

---

**Step 4: Map Each Feature to a Causal Variable**

| # | High-Performing Feature | Causal Variable | Practical Mechanism |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Mutual Trust & Confidence | Leadership Process | Psychological safety, no blame culture |
| 2 | Participative Decision-Making | Decision Process | Voting, consensus, brainstorming |
| 3 | Group-Based Supervision | Influence Process | Linking pin, team structure |
| 4 | High Performance Goals | Goal-Setting Process | Stretch targets, OKRs |
| 5 | Free Flow of Information | Communication Process | Town halls, transparent dashboards |
| 6 | Supportive Relationships | Motivation Process | Coaching, mentoring, recognition |
| 7 | Overlapping Work Groups | Structure | Matrix structure, cross-functional teams |

---

**Step 5: Operationalize the Model**

For an organization to transition from System 1 / 2 / 3 to the Supportive Model, it must deliberately alter its **causal variables** in the following sequence:

$$
\text{Step A: Build Trust} \rightarrow \text{Step B: Enable Participation} \rightarrow \text{Step C: Restructure into Overlapping Groups} \rightarrow \text{Step D: Establish Joint Goal-Setting} \rightarrow \text{Step E: Decentralize Control} \rightarrow \text{Step F: Institutionalize Linking Pin}
$$

This sequence ensures that changes are not disruptive but cumulative — each stage creates the psychological and structural foundation for the next.

---

**Step 6: Verify with Performance Outcomes**

The Supportive Model predicts that, when properly implemented:

$$
\uparrow \text{Trust} + \uparrow \text{Participation} + \uparrow \text{Group Decisions} \implies \uparrow \text{Productivity}, \uparrow \text{Quality}, \uparrow \text{Morale}, \downarrow \text{Turnover}, \downarrow \text{Conflict}
$$

Empirical studies by Likert and his successors (such as those published in *New Patterns of Management* and *The Human Organization*) consistently validated this prediction.

---

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Supportive Model vs. Other Likert Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked comparative question in KTU exams. Memorize the dimensions and the contrast direction.**

| Dimension | System 1 (Exploitative) | System 2 (Benevolent) | System 3 (Consultative) | **System 4 (Supportive)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Trust** | None | Conditional, condescending | Substantial but incomplete | **Complete trust in subordinates** |
| **Communication** | Downward only, distorted | Mostly downward | Downward + limited upward | **All directions, accurate** |
| **Decisions** | Top-down | Top-down with mercy | Top-down with consultation | **Group decision-making** |
| **Motivation** | Fear, threats | Economic rewards | Rewards + some involvement | **Participation, growth, self-actualization** |
| **Control** | Centralized | Centralized | Moderately decentralized | **Decentralized, self-control** |
| **Goals** | Low / imposed | Low / imposed | Moderate / consulted | **High / jointly set** |
| **Subordinate Influence** | None | Minimal | Moderate | **Substantial** |
| **Performance Level** | Low | Low-Moderate | Moderate-High | **High** |
| **Human Outcome** | Poor satisfaction | Limited satisfaction | Moderate satisfaction | **High satisfaction + growth** |

---

### 3.3 The Manager's Functional Role in the Supportive Model

Likert argued that the manager's job in a Supportive organization is fundamentally different from that in a System 1 or 2 organization. The manager performs three primary functions:

$$
\text{Manager's Role} = \{F_1, F_2, F_3\}
$$

Where:
- $F_1$ = **Supportive Function** — Help subordinates reach their highest level of motivation by removing obstacles, providing resources, and creating a climate of trust.
- $F_2$ = **Group-Function** — Help establish and maintain effective work groups, ensuring they have clear goals, sound structure, and adequate resources.
- $F_3$ = **Conflict-Resolution Function** — Help resolve interpersonal and intergroup conflicts through open dialogue, mutual respect, and consensus-building, rather than through authority.

The mathematical representation of organizational effectiveness under the Supportive Model is:

$$
\text{Effectiveness} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i \times G_i \times E_i)
$$

Where:
- $M_i$ = Motivation level of employee $i$ (high in Supportive Model)
- $G_i$ = Group synergy contribution of employee $i$ (high in Supportive Model)
- $E_i$ = Effort exerted by employee $i$ (high in Supportive Model)
- $n$ = Number of employees

> [!NOTE]
> **The above formula is a conceptual representation, not a precise mathematical equation.** In Likert's framework, the key insight is that high motivation multiplied by high group synergy multiplied by high effort produces **multiplicative** gains — not merely additive ones. This is why the Supportive Model outperforms Systems 1-3 on almost every metric.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define the Supportive Model of management. Who proposed it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Supportive Model** (also called **Likert's Management System 4** or **Participative Group Management**) is a leadership and organizational framework proposed by **Rensis Likert**, based on his research at the University of Michigan's Institute for Social Research. It is founded on the principle that an organization's performance depends on the quality of human resources and the extent to which managerial practices foster **participative, trust-based, and group-oriented work relationships**. The model emphasizes mutual trust, open communication, group decision-making, joint goal-setting, and the use of overlapping work groups connected through the **"linking pin"** mechanism. It seeks to achieve both **high productivity** and **high employee satisfaction** simultaneously, viewing the two as complementary rather than conflicting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the "Linking Pin" concept in the Supportive Model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **"Linking Pin"** concept was introduced by **Rensis Likert** as the structural mechanism that enables group decision-making in the Supportive Model. A linking pin is a person (typically a supervisor) who holds **membership in two or more overlapping work groups** — one group at their own hierarchical level and another at an adjacent level (above or below). This dual membership allows the linking pin to **transmit information, decisions, and values vertically and laterally** throughout the organization, ensuring **seamless coordination** and **free flow of communication** across all levels. The linking pin transforms the rigid chain-of-command into an **interlocking web of influence** that is essential for the Supportive Model's participative philosophy.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
#### **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the key features of the Supportive Model of management with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast the Supportive Model with the Exploitative Authoritative and Benevolent Authoritative models of management. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer for Q(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Supportive Model (Likert's System 4) is the most participative of Likert's four management systems. It is built on the premise that people are motivated by higher-order needs and that organizational effectiveness is maximized when subordinates are involved in decisions affecting their work.

**Key Features (5 marks — one feature with example for each mark):**

1. **Mutual Trust and Confidence:** Managers exhibit complete trust in their subordinates. *Example:* In a software firm, a project manager openly shares project risks with the team and trusts them to self-organize sprints without micromanagement.

2. **Group Decision-Making:** Decisions are made through group processes, not unilateral commands. *Example:* A cross-functional product team collectively decides the roadmap priorities in a quarterly planning meeting.

3. **Free Flow of Communication:** Information flows in all directions — upward, downward, and lateral — without distortion. *Example:* An engineering firm holds monthly "skip-level" town halls where junior engineers can directly ask the CEO questions.

4. **High Performance Goals:** Goals are jointly set and stretch the organization's capabilities. *Example:* An automobile company sets a "zero-defect" target jointly with its assembly-line workers through quality circles.

5. **Supportive Supervision:** Managers act as coaches and facilitators, removing obstacles. *Example:* A research lab manager provides lab equipment, software licenses, and mentoring to enable scientists to focus on innovation.

6. **Linking Pin Structure:** Supervisors are members of both their own group and their superior's group, enabling vertical integration. *Example:* A team lead attends both the engineering management meeting and the developer stand-up, bridging strategy with execution.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The Supportive Model produces a synergistic combination of high productivity and high morale, making it Likert's ideal management system for modern organizations.

---

#### Model Answer for Q(b):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Rensis Likert classified organizations into four management systems based on their leadership style, communication, motivation, and decision-making approach. The following comparison highlights the philosophical gap between the Supportive Model and the two authoritarian systems.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Dimension | Exploitative Authoritative (System 1) | Benevolent Authoritative (System 2) | **Supportive (System 4)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Trust** | No trust in subordinates | Conditional, condescending trust | **Complete trust in subordinates** |
| **Communication** | Downward only, heavily distorted | Mostly downward, limited feedback | **Free flow in all directions** |
| **Decision-Making** | Top-down, unilateral | Top-down with master-servant grace | **Group decision-making** |
| **Motivation** | Fear, threats, economic penalties | Economic rewards, paternal care | **Participation, growth, self-actualization** |
| **Goal-Setting** | Orders issued from above | Goals set with limited consultation | **Jointly set, high performance goals** |
| **Control** | Centralized, policing | Centralized with some delegation | **Decentralized, self-control** |
| **Performance Outcome** | Low productivity, high conflict | Low-moderate productivity, moderate satisfaction | **High productivity, high satisfaction** |

**Critical Analysis (1 mark):**
While System 1 and 2 may yield short-term compliance, they suppress innovation, increase turnover, and create a culture of fear. The Supportive Model, in contrast, generates sustainable performance by aligning organizational goals with individual growth needs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain Likert's classification of management systems. Discuss the characteristic features of the Supportive Model in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** Critically evaluate the Supportive Model. What are its strengths and limitations? (7 marks)

#### Model Answer for Q(a):

**Likert's Classification (3 marks):**
Rensis Likert, after extensive research, classified organizations into four management systems:
- **System 1 — Exploitative Authoritative:** Autocratic, fear-driven, top-down.
- **System 2 — Benevolent Authoritative:** Paternalistic, master-servant relationship.
- **System 3 — Consultative:** Two-way communication but decisions remain with managers.
- **System 4 — Supportive (Participative Group):** Fully participative, trust-based.

**Characteristic Features of the Supportive Model (4 marks):**
1. Managers have **complete confidence and trust** in subordinates.
2. Subordinates feel **free to discuss** job-related problems with superiors.
3. Ideas and suggestions are **freely exchanged**.
4. Decisions are made through **group processes** in overlapping groups.
5. There is **substantial interaction** between superiors and subordinates.
6. Subordinates are motivated by **participation and self-actualization**.
7. Goals are **jointly set** and are **high-performance goals**.
8. Control is **decentralized**, and the **linking pin** structure enables vertical integration.

---

#### Model Answer for Q(b):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Supportive Model is widely regarded as the most effective management system, but it is not without limitations. A critical evaluation must consider both its strengths and the practical challenges of implementation.

**Strengths (3 marks):**
1. **Higher Productivity and Morale:** Organizations using System 4 consistently outperform others in productivity, quality, and employee satisfaction.
2. **Innovation and Adaptability:** Participative decision-making fosters creativity, as diverse viewpoints are integrated.
3. **Lower Conflict and Turnover:** Trust-based relationships reduce interpersonal conflict and voluntary attrition.
4. **Employee Growth:** Subordinates develop leadership skills through continuous participation in group decisions.
5. **Alignment of Individual and Organizational Goals:** Self-actualization through work aligns personal aspirations with corporate objectives.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Time-Consuming Decision-Making:** Group decisions take longer than autocratic commands, which may be unsuitable in crisis situations.
2. **Not Universally Applicable:** Some industries (e.g., military, emergency services) require centralized command for operational necessity.
3. **Requires Mature Workforce:** The model assumes that subordinates are skilled, knowledgeable, and willing to participate — this is not always the case, especially with unskilled labour.
4. **Resistance to Change:** Transitioning from System 1 or 2 to System 4 requires deep cultural change, which may encounter resistance from both managers (losing power) and subordinates (unwilling to take responsibility).
5. **Risk of Groupthink:** Over-emphasis on consensus may suppress dissenting opinions and lead to poor decisions.
6. **Difficult to Measure:** Soft variables like trust and morale are hard to quantify, making ROI on supportive practices difficult to demonstrate to top leadership.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Despite its limitations, the Supportive Model remains the most empirically validated framework for achieving sustainable organizational excellence. Its successful implementation requires a deliberate, long-term commitment to cultural transformation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Don't confuse "Supportive Model" with "Servant Leadership" or "Transformational Leadership."** While they share philosophical overlaps, the Supportive Model is *specifically* Likert's System 4. Mixing up the authors (Greenleaf for Servant Leadership, Burns/Bass for Transformational) will cost you marks.
>
> 2. **Don't list features without examples.** A KTU 14-mark question expects *at least 5-6 features* with an example or practical application for each. A feature without an example is worth only partial credit.
>
> 3. **Don't write essays on the other three systems.** When asked to compare, spend **at most 2-3 lines** on System 1 and 2 each. The bulk of your answer must focus on the Supportive Model.
>
> 4. **Don't forget the "Linking Pin" concept.** This is a high-frequency KTU question. Always draw a small diagram if the question is worth ≥ 10 marks.
>
> 5. **Don't omit the limitations in a "critically evaluate" question.** Writing only strengths will cap your marks at 60-70% of the total. The word "critically" demands a balanced view.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Supportive Model** is **Likert's System 4** (Participative Group Management), developed based on research at the **University of Michigan's Institute for Social Research**.
- The model operates on the principle of **mutual trust, open communication, group decision-making, and joint goal-setting**.
- It is the **most participative** of Likert's four systems and is empirically linked to **the highest organizational performance**.
- The **Linking Pin** concept is the structural backbone of the Supportive Model — a supervisor belongs to both their own group and their superior's group.
- The model uses a **Causal → Intervening → End-Result** variable chain to explain how managerial practices translate into performance outcomes.
- The manager's role is **supportive, facilitative, and group-building**, not directive.
- The Supportive Model is most effective in **mature, knowledge-economy organizations** (software firms, R&D labs, design studios).
- Its limitations include **slower decision-making, groupthink risk, and difficulty in implementation** in rigidly hierarchical settings.
- **Memory hook:** Supportive Model = **3T Formula** (Trust, Teamwork, Transparent communication) + **Linking Pin** structure + **Joint goal-setting**.
- **Always mention the author (Rensis Likert)** in the first sentence of any KTU answer on this topic.
- **Always include at least one example** (preferably from software/IT/engineering) in a 7-mark or 14-mark question to score full marks.
- **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark questions — specifically the **Linking Pin structure** or the **Four Systems comparison pyramid**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collegial Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** UEHUT803 - ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOUR AND BUSINESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Collegial Model # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Collegial Model** of Organizational Behaviour is the fourth stage in Keith Davis's classical evolutionary model of OB. It is a people-oriented framework built on the premise of **partnership, teamwork, and shared authority**, in which management creates a work environment where employees derive intrinsic motivation from their competence, contribution, and sense of belonging to a professional community. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 scheme, the Collegial Model represents a shift from *directive control* (Autocratic) and *economic incentives* (Custodial) towards *intrinsic job satisfaction* and *social-psychological fulfillment*. It lays the foundation for the later **System Model** by emphasizing mutual trust, respect, and collaborative decision-making. In this model, the manager's role is redefined from a controller to a **facilitator, coordinator, and team leader**. Employees are treated as *colleagues* or *partners* working together toward shared organizational objectives, rather than subordinates executing orders. ### Key Philosophical Premise $$ \text{Organizational Effectiveness} = f(\text{Partnership} \times \text{Shared Authority} \times \text{Mutual Trust}) $$ The model assumes that individuals in modern organizations have *higher-order psychological needs* (such as esteem, self-actualization, and social belonging) that cannot be satisfied by economic rewards alone — they must be met through meaningful participation and professional recognition. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Surgical Team"** > > Imagine a cardiac surgery team in a multi-specialty hospital. The lead surgeon does not "order" the anesthetist to put the patient to sleep — they are **colleagues** with specialized expertise collaborating on a shared mission. Each member has a defined role, mutual respect, deep trust, and the collective achievement of saving the patient's life is the team's success. > > - The **Head Surgeon** = *Manager* (acts as coordinator, not commander) > - The **Anesthetist / Nurses** = *Colleague-Employees* (professionally skilled, autonomous) > - The **Operating Theatre** = *Work Environment* (high mutual trust, open communication) > - **Saving the Patient** = *Organizational Objective* (shared mission) > > This is the *spirit* of the Collegial Model. People perform not because they fear punishment (Autocratic) or seek only money (Custodial), but because they want to *contribute meaningfully* to a cause they believe in, alongside people they respect. A simpler analogy: **think of a university research lab** where professors, research scholars, and lab assistants collaborate. Hierarchy exists, but authority is *consultative* and expertise-driven. This collegial atmosphere drives innovation far more than rigid command would. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Operational Parameters | Parameter | Standard Value in Collegial Model | |---|---| | **Orientation** | People-oriented + Team-oriented | | **Manager's Authority** | Shared, consultative, distributed | | **Employee Status Source** | Competence and contribution (not rank) | | **Need Focus** | Self-actualization, esteem, belonging | | **Communication** | Two-way, open, lateral + vertical | | **Conflict Resolution** | Consensus, peer negotiation, dialogue | | **Employee Morale** | High (intrinsically motivated) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remember:** The word *Collegial* comes from the Latin *collega* meaning "partner" or "one chosen to work alongside." The model emphasizes that every person in the organization is, in a sense, a *co-worker* on a shared mission. --- ## 1.4 Historical Context (Evolutionary Position) The Collegial Model was formally introduced by **Keith Davis** in the 1960s–70s as an extension of the earlier OB models. It occupies a *transitional* position in the evolutionary chain: $$ \text{Autocratic} \rightarrow \text{Custodial} \rightarrow \text{Supportive} \rightarrow \color{red}{\text{Collegial}} \rightarrow \text{System} $$ Each model represents a higher level of *humanization* of the workplace. The Collegial Model, in particular, was a response to the growing educational and professional sophistication of the post-WWII workforce, where employees demanded more voice and dignity at work. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations The Collegial Model is anchored in three major psychological theories. Understanding these is *high-yield* for KTU exams: ### 2.1.1 Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (Top Two Tiers) The model directly addresses the **higher-order needs** of the human personality: $$ \text{Needs}_{\text{Collegial}} = \big\{ \text{Esteem Needs},\ \text{Self-Actualization Needs} \big\} $$ - **Esteem Needs** → satisfied by being recognized as a competent partner - **Self-Actualization** → satisfied by meaningful contribution to challenging work ### 2.1.2 McGregor's Theory Y (Democratic Style) The model assumes *people are self-motivated, creative, and seek responsibility* — exactly the assumptions of McGregor's **Theory Y**. Managers therefore adopt a *democratic, participative* leadership style. ### 2.1.3 Likert's System 4 (Participative Group) Rensis Likert's research identified four management systems. The Collegial Model aligns with his **System 4 — Participative Group**, characterized by: - Group decision-making - Overlapping superior-subordinate relationships - High performance aspirations - Mutual trust --- ## 2.2 Structural Components of the Collegial Model The model rests on **three pillars** that must be in balance for it to function effectively: ### Pillar 1: Partnership - People view themselves as **partners**, not subordinates. - *Why:* Creates ownership and shared purpose. - *How:* Flattened hierarchy, open-door policies, cross-functional teams. ### Pillar 2: Shared Authority & Mutual Trust - Authority is *consultative* — decisions are made jointly. - *Why:* Decisions are more robust and employees are more committed. - *How:* Committees, councils, quality circles, and self-managed teams. ### Pillar 3: Contribution Orientation - Employees are driven by the *impact* of their work. - *Why:* Intrinsic motivation is more sustainable than extrinsic. - *How:* Stretch assignments, professional development, autonomy in task execution. --- ## 2.3 Behavioural Outcomes in the Collegial Model | Dimension | Collegial Behaviour | |---|---| | **Employee Attitude** | Committed, engaged, professional | | **Motivation Type** | Intrinsic (achievement, recognition, work itself) | | **Performance** | High quality, innovative | | **Conflict** | Resolved through dialogue and consensus | | **Communication** | Lateral, open, multi-directional | | **Compliance** | Internalized (because people believe in the goal) | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Concept Equivalents (Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams):** > > | Concept | Collegial Equivalent | > |---|---| > | --- | --- | > | Manager's role | Coordinator / Facilitator / Team leader | > | Employee's role | Colleague / Partner / Expert contributor | > | Need satisfied | Higher-order needs (Esteem, Self-actualization) | > | Authority flow | Shared and consultative | > | Communication | Multi-directional and open | > | Motivation driver | Intrinsic (work itself) | > | Control mechanism | Self-discipline and peer accountability | > | Underlying theory | Maslow (top tiers), McGregor (Theory Y), Likert (System 4) | > | Stage number | 4th of 5 models (Autocratic → Custodial → Supportive → Collegial → System) | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Model in Engineering & Tech Organizations The Collegial Model is *especially* relevant to the modern engineering workplace: 1. **R&D Labs and Innovation Centers** — Engineers, scientists, and designers must collaborate as peers to innovate. 2. **Software Development Teams** — Agile/Scrum teams operate on collegial principles: self-organization, shared ownership, mutual respect. 3. **Consulting Firms** — Partners, senior consultants, and analysts work as colleagues on client projects. 4. **Academic Institutions** — Faculty, researchers, and administrators are collegial in spirit. 5. **Cross-Functional Product Teams** — Engineers, designers, marketers, and product managers function as *interdisciplinary colleagues*. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Industrial Insight:** Many Kerala-based IT firms (e.g., TCS Technopark, Infosys, UST Global) and engineering R&D centers in the KTU ecosystem explicitly cultivate collegial cultures to retain top engineering talent and foster innovation. The "open-door culture" and "flat hierarchy" you see in these companies are direct applications of the Collegial Model. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown ## 3.1 Comparative Derivation: How the Collegial Model Evolved To fully understand the Collegial Model, we must derive it from the *preceding* models. Each prior model had a *limitation* that the next model corrected. ### Step 1: Autocratic Model — The Starting Point The earliest OB model was based on *power and obedience*: $$ \text{Compliance}_{\text{Autocratic}} = f(\text{Fear},\ \text{Authority of Manager}) $$ - **Strength:** Quick decision-making. - **Limitation:** Low morale, high turnover, dependency. ### Step 2: Custodial Model — The Economic Fix Industrial-era thinkers realized that fear alone was unsustainable. They introduced *economic security*: $$ \text{Loyalty}_{\text{Custodial}} = f(\text{Monetary Rewards},\ \text{Job Security}) $$ - **Strength:** Stability, satisfied lower-order needs. - **Limitation:** Did not address *psychological* or *self-actualization* needs. Workers stayed but did not *contribute passionately*. ### Step 3: Supportive Model — The First Psychological Leap The next evolution recognized that *people need more than money*: $$ \text{Motivation}_{\text{Supportive}} = f(\text{Leadership Support},\ \text{Recognition}) $$ - **Strength:** Tapped into *social and esteem needs*. - **Limitation:** Still operated within a *traditional hierarchy* — employees remained subordinates, not partners. ### Step 4: Collegial Model — The Partnership Breakthrough The Collegial Model directly addresses the *limitation* of the Supportive Model by introducing **partnership**: $$ \boxed{\;\text{Effectiveness}_{\text{Collegial}} = f(\text{Partnership},\ \text{Shared Authority},\ \text{Mutual Trust},\ \text{Intrinsic Motivation})\;} $$ **Conversion Logic (Textual Explanation):** - The Supportive Model treats employees as *subordinates* who need support. - The Collegial Model elevates them to *colleagues* who *co-own* the mission. - Authority becomes a *shared resource* rather than a *positional attribute*. - Status flows from *expertise and contribution*, not *rank or seniority*. - The manager transitions from a *controller* to a *facilitator-coordinator*. ### Step 5: Transition to System Model (Preview) The Collegial Model eventually evolves into the **System Model** when the entire organization becomes an integrated, self-regulating ecosystem. The Collegial Model is, therefore, the *bridge* between the humanistic-supportive philosophy and the fully integrated systems view. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Five-Stage Model Comparison The following table is the *single most important comparative* for KTU exams on this topic: | Dimension | Autocratic | Custodial | Supportive | **Collegial** | System | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Basis | Power | Economic | Leadership | **Partnership** | Resources | | Manager role | Boss | Benevolent | Facilitator | **Coordinator** | Catalyser | | Employee view | Subordinate | Trusted servant | Capable partner | **Colleague** | Whole person | | Need satisfied | Physiological, Safety | Security, Belonging | Social, Esteem | **Esteem, Self-actualization** | All five | | Control type | Direct | Indirect (rules) | Internalized | **Self & peer** | Self-regulating | | Communication | Top-down | Top-down | Both ways | **Multi-directional** | Networked | | Morale | Low-medium | Medium | High | **Very high** | Highest | | Innovation | Low | Low | Medium | **High** | Very high | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse the *Supportive* and *Collegial* models. Remember: **Supportive = supportive leadership over subordinates**; **Collegial = partnership among equals**. The keyword that distinguishes them is *"partnership."* --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Application of the Model to an Engineering Scenario **Scenario:** A multidisciplinary team at a KTU-affiliated engineering college is building an autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) for a national competition. | Step | Collegial Element in Action | |---|---| | **Step 1: Team Formation** | Mechanical, electronics, and computer science students form a *cross-functional team of peers* (Partnership). | | **Step 2: Shared Mission** | The team defines a *shared objective* — win the competition with a high-performing AUV. | | **Step 3: Distributed Authority** | The faculty mentor is a *facilitator*, not a commander. Each domain lead has *technical authority* over their subsystem. | | **Step 4: Mutual Trust** | Subsystem teams *trust* that other teams will deliver on their interfaces. | | **Step 5: Open Communication** | Daily stand-up meetings, shared documentation, peer code reviews — all *multi-directional communication*. | | **Step 6: Intrinsic Motivation** | Team members are driven by the *challenge, learning, and recognition* of competing — not merely by grades. | | **Step 7: Collective Achievement** | Success is attributed to the *team*, not to a single leader — a hallmark of collegial culture. | This example maps directly onto the Collegial Model's principles and is an excellent answer structure for KTU application-based questions. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Collegial Model Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A1[Autocratic Model
Power-Based] --> B1[Custodial Model
Economic-Based] B1 --> C1[Supportive Model
Leadership-Based] C1 --> D1[Collegial Model
Partnership-Based] D1 --> E1[System Model
Resource-Based] D1 --> P1[Pillar 1: Partnership] D1 --> P2[Pillar 2: Shared Authority] D1 --> P3[Pillar 3: Mutual Trust] D1 --> P4[Pillar 4: Contribution Orientation] P1 --> O1[Employee as Colleague] P2 --> O2[Manager as Coordinator] P3 --> O3[Open Multi-Directional Communication] P4 --> O4[Intrinsic Motivation from Work] O1 --> R1[High Morale and Engagement] O2 --> R2[Empowered Workforce] O3 --> R3[Reduced Conflict and Trust] O4 --> R4[Innovation and Quality Output] R1 --> F1[Organizational Effectiveness] R2 --> F1 R3 --> F1 R4 --> F1 style D1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style P1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** The diagram illustrates the evolutionary position of the Collegial Model and its four supporting pillars. The yellow-highlighted node (D1) is the focal point. All four pillars converge into four behavioural outcomes, which together produce **organizational effectiveness** — the terminal outcome. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Comparative Manager Role Across Models ```mermaid graph LR M1[Autocratic Manager
BOSS] -->|Power| E1[Subordinate Employee] M2[Custodial Manager
BENEVOLENT PATRON] -->|Security| E2[Trusted Servant] M3[Supportive Manager
HELPER] -->|Guidance| E3[Capable Subordinate] M4[Collegial Manager
COORDINATOR] -->|Partnership| E4[Colleague Employee] M5[System Manager
CATALYSER] -->|Integration| E5[Whole Person] style M4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style E4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Need-Satisfaction Flow in the Collegial Model ```mermaid graph TD N1[Lower-Order Needs
Met by Prior Models] --> N2[Esteem Needs
Recognition and Respect] N2 --> N3[Self-Actualization
Contribution and Growth] N3 --> N4[Intrinsic Job Satisfaction] N4 --> N5[Sustained High Performance] N2 -.Satisfied by.-> C1[Being Treated as Partner] N3 -.Satisfied by.-> C2[Meaningful and Challenging Work] N4 -.Sustained by.-> C3[Mutual Trust and Collegial Culture] N5 -.Reinforced by.-> C4[Team Achievement and Recognition] style N4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style N5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Practical Application in an Engineering Team ```mermaid graph TD T1[Cross-Functional Engineering Team] --> T2[Mechanical Lead] T1 --> T3[Electronics Lead] T1 --> T4[Software Lead] T1 --> T5[Faculty Mentor as Coordinator] T2 --> R1[Subsystem Delivery: Hull and Propulsion] T3 --> R2[Subsystem Delivery: Sensors and Control] T4 --> R3[Subsystem Delivery: Navigation and AI] T5 --> F1[Facilitates, Does Not Command] R1 --> P1[Integrated Autonomous Vehicle] R2 --> P1 R3 --> P1 F1 --> P1 P1 --> OUT1[Competition Success and Team Satisfaction] style T1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style T5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style OUT1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **Q: Define the Collegial Model of Organizational Behaviour. List its three key features.** **Model Answer:** The Collegial Model of Organizational Behaviour, introduced by Keith Davis, is a people-oriented framework in which employees are treated as **partners or colleagues** working together with management toward shared organizational objectives. It is based on the premise that people derive deep satisfaction from *meaningful contribution, mutual respect, and shared authority*, rather than from fear or economic security alone. **Three Key Features:** 1. **Partnership Orientation** — Employees and managers view each other as colleagues with a shared mission, not as superior and subordinate. 2. **Shared and Consultative Authority** — Decision-making is decentralized; managers act as *facilitators and coordinators* rather than controllers. 3. **Higher-Order Need Satisfaction** — The model satisfies *esteem* and *self-actualization* needs, leading to intrinsically motivated employees and high morale. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **Q: How does the Collegial Model differ from the Supportive Model of OB?** **Model Answer:** | Basis of Difference | Supportive Model | Collegial Model | |---|---|---| | **Relationship** | Manager is a *supporter* of subordinates | Manager is a *partner* of colleagues | | **Authority** | Remains with the manager (delegated) | Authority is *shared* and consultative | | **Need Satisfied** | Social and lower esteem needs | Higher esteem and self-actualization | | **Employee Status** | Subordinate with support | Colleague with partnership | | **Communication** | Mostly vertical (both ways) | Multi-directional, including lateral | In essence, the Supportive Model gives employees *support within a hierarchy*, whereas the Collegial Model *redesigns the relationship itself* to be one of equals working together. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Extended-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **Q (a): Explain the Collegial Model of Organizational Behaviour in detail. Discuss the underlying psychological theories that support it.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Definition and Premise (2 marks):** The Collegial Model is the fourth stage in Keith Davis's evolutionary model of OB. It is built on the **premise of partnership** — that employees and managers work as colleagues toward a shared mission. The model assumes that modern, educated, and professional employees are motivated by intrinsic rewards like contribution, recognition, and self-actualization, not merely by economic security or directives. **2. Key Pillars of the Collegial Model (2 marks):** - **Partnership:** Flattened hierarchy, open-door culture, and team-based working. - **Shared Authority:** Decisions are consultative; the manager is a coordinator. - **Mutual Trust:** Communication is multi-directional; employees self-regulate. **3. Underlying Psychological Theories (3 marks):** (i) **Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs:** The Collegial Model directly addresses the *top two tiers* — *Esteem Needs* (recognition, respect) and *Self-Actualization* (growth, meaningful contribution). By involving employees as partners, the model satisfies these higher-order needs. (ii) **McGregor's Theory Y:** The model is rooted in Theory Y assumptions — that people are *self-motivated, creative, responsible, and seek to contribute*. The manager's role is to *facilitate*, not to coerce. (iii) **Likert's System 4 (Participative Group):** Likert identified that organizations with high trust, group decision-making, and overlapping superior-subordinate relationships outperform command-driven ones. The Collegial Model embodies System 4 principles. **4. Behavioural Outcomes:** High morale, intrinsic motivation, innovation, low turnover, strong organizational commitment, and superior performance quality. --- **Q (b): "The Collegial Model represents a higher level of humanization of the workplace." Critically analyze this statement with examples from engineering organizations.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Meaning of "Humanization of the Workplace" (2 marks):** Humanization refers to the progressive recognition of employees as *whole persons* with psychological, social, and self-actualization needs — not just as economic units. The Collegial Model is a significant step in this direction because it elevates employees from *subordinates* to *partners*. **2. Evidence of Higher Humanization (3 marks):** - **From Control to Collaboration:** Earlier models treated people as *inputs* to be controlled; the Collegial Model treats them as *co-creators* of value. - **From Subordination to Partnership:** Status flows from *competence*, not rank — a deeply humanizing shift. - **From External Rewards to Intrinsic Motivation:** The model addresses the *human need for purpose* and *meaningful work*, going beyond economic transactions. - **From Top-Down Communication to Open Dialogue:** Employees' voices are heard and respected — a hallmark of dignified workplace treatment. **3. Engineering Organization Examples (2 marks):** - **Agile Software Teams in IT firms (e.g., Infosys, TCS, UST Global):** Cross-functional teams operate as *colleagues*, with self-organization, peer reviews, and shared product ownership. This is a direct application of the Collegial Model. - **R&D Centers in engineering companies (e.g., Bosch, Tata Elxsi, DRDO labs):** Scientists and engineers collaborate as peers, with research leads acting as coordinators rather than commanders. **4. Critical Perspective (additional credit):** While the Collegial Model is highly humanizing, it has *limitations* — it requires mature employees, may slow decisions in crises, and depends heavily on trust. In *highly regulated* or *safety-critical* engineering environments (e.g., nuclear plants), pure collegiality may be insufficient and must be balanced with clear authority. **Conclusion:** The Collegial Model does represent a *genuine* advancement in workplace humanization, but its effectiveness depends on the *cultural maturity* of the organization and the *nature of the work*. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **Q (a): Compare the Collegial Model with the Autocratic and Custodial Models of Organizational Behaviour. Why is the Collegial Model considered more suitable for modern engineering organizations?** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Three-Way Comparison (4 marks):** | Dimension | Autocratic | Custodial | Collegial | |---|---|---|---| | **Basis** | Power | Economic resources | Partnership | | **Manager's Role** | Boss / Commander | Benevolent patron | Coordinator / Facilitator | | **Employee View** | Subordinate | Trusted servant | Colleague / Partner | | **Need Satisfied** | Lower-order (safety) | Security and belonging | Esteem and self-actualization | | **Communication** | Top-down | Top-down | Multi-directional | | **Morale** | Low | Moderate | High | | **Control** | Direct (rules) | Indirect (economic) | Self / peer | | **Innovation** | Low | Low | High | **2. Why Collegial Model Suits Modern Engineering Organizations (3 marks):** (i) **Highly Educated Workforce:** Modern engineers are professionals with deep expertise. They respond better to *consultation and partnership* than to *commands*. (ii) **Innovation-Driven Work:** Engineering innovation (e.g., R&D, product design) requires *creativity*, which flourishes in *autonomous, collegial* environments, not in rigid hierarchies. (iii) **Cross-Functional Collaboration:** Modern engineering projects (e.g., AI, robotics, EV design) require collaboration across mechanical, electronics, software, and data science domains — a *collegial partnership* model fits this naturally. (iv) **Retention of Talent:** Talented engineers value *respect, voice, and professional growth* — all delivered by the Collegial Model. Companies that fail to provide this lose talent to competitors. (v) **Agile and Self-Managed Teams:** Contemporary engineering practices (Agile, Scrum, DevOps) explicitly use *self-organizing teams* — a real-world application of the Collegial Model. --- **Q (b): Discuss the limitations of the Collegial Model and the conditions required for its successful implementation.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Limitations of the Collegial Model (3.5 marks):** (i) **Requires Highly Mature Employees:** The model assumes employees are self-motivated professionals. In organizations with low skill levels or low engagement, the model fails. (ii) **Slow Decision-Making:** Consensus-based decisions take time. In *crisis situations* (e.g., production breakdowns, safety emergencies), collegial deliberation can cause delays. (iii) **Dependency on Trust:** If mutual trust breaks down (e.g., due to layoffs, ethical violations), the model collapses quickly. (iv) **Authority Vacuum:** With authority diffused, there can be *role confusion* and *accountability gaps* — a risk in safety-critical engineering. (v) **Cultural Fit:** In hierarchical societies or organizations, the model may face *cultural resistance* from both managers (reluctant to share authority) and subordinates (unaccustomed to taking initiative). (vi) **Unequal Skill Distribution:** In teams where one member is *significantly more skilled*, the appearance of "collegiality" can be a façade, with the skilled member effectively dominating. **2.
