Conditions for Successful Implementation (3.5 marks):** (i) **Strong Organizational Culture:** The organization must have a *value system* that genuinely respects individuals and promotes trust. (ii) **Competent Workforce:** Employees must have the *education, training, and skills* to contribute as peers. (iii) **Transformational Leadership:** Managers must be trained to act as *facilitators and coaches*, not controllers. (iv) **Open Communication Systems:** Formal and informal channels for *lateral and multi-directional* communication must exist. (v) **Equitable Reward Systems:** Recognition and rewards must be *merit-based*, not position-based. (vi) **Decentralized Decision Structure:** Decisions should be pushed to the *lowest competent level*, empowering teams. (vii) **Selection and Socialization:** Hiring should focus on *cultural fit* — people who value teamwork, openness, and contribution. **Conclusion:** The Collegial Model is a powerful framework, but it is not universally applicable. It works best in *knowledge-intensive, professionally-driven, innovation-oriented* organizations — a perfect fit for many modern engineering and technology enterprises. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on this Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing Supportive and Collegial models** — The single most common error. Remember: *Supportive = supportive leader + capable subordinate*; *Collegial = partnership among colleagues*. If you write "manager is supportive" for the Collegial Model, you have described the Supportive Model, not Collegial. > > 2. **Forgetting the theoretical anchors** — KTU examiners reward answers that cite **Maslow (top tiers), McGregor (Theory Y), and Likert (System 4)**. A purely descriptive answer without these references will lose 2–3 marks. > > 3. **Ignoring the evolutionary context** — Always place the Collegial Model in its *fourth-stage* position in the Davis model. Examiners expect a comparative reference. > > 4. **No real-world examples** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *application*. At least one engineering/IT example (Agile teams, R&D labs, cross-functional product teams) is mandatory for a full-marks answer. > > 5. **One-sided answers** — The model has *limitations*. A complete answer should mention at least 2–3 limitations and conditions for success. > > 6. **Confusing the manager's role** — The Collegial Manager is a *coordinator/facilitator*, NOT a boss or a supporter. Use the exact word "coordinator" or "facilitator" in exams. > > 7. **Mixing up Custodial and Supportive needs** — Custodial = *security/economic*; Supportive = *social/lower esteem*; Collegial = *higher esteem/self-actualization*. Getting this ladder wrong is a guaranteed mark loss. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU 2024 Exam:** > > - **Collegial Model = Partnership + Shared Authority + Mutual Trust** *(Memorize this triad)* > - **Stage Number:** 4th of 5 models (Autocratic → Custodial → Supportive → **Collegial** → System) > - **Proposed by:** Keith Davis > - **Manager's role:** Coordinator / Facilitator / Team leader (NOT boss, NOT supporter) > - **Employee's role:** Colleague / Partner / Expert contributor > - **Need satisfied:** Higher-order — *Esteem* and *Self-Actualization* (top of Maslow's pyramid) > - **Underlying theories:** Maslow (top tiers), McGregor (Theory Y), Likert (System 4) > - **Status source:** Competence and contribution, NOT rank > - **Communication:** Multi-directional, open, lateral + vertical > - **Control mechanism:** Self-discipline and peer accountability > - **Motivation type:** Intrinsic (achievement, recognition, work itself) > - **Key distinguishing word:** *PARTNERSHIP* (this single word separates Collegial from all other models) > - **Real-world fit:** R&D labs, Agile software teams, cross-functional engineering projects, academic institutions > - **Limitations to mention in exams:** Requires mature workforce, slow decision-making, depends on trust, may not suit crisis or safety-critical contexts > - **Conditions for success:** Strong culture, competent workforce, transformational leaders, open communication, merit-based rewards > - **Examiner's golden phrase:** Always end answers with a one-line conclusion linking back to *partnership, shared authority, and self-actualization*. > [!NOTE] > **Final Memory Aid — The "P-S-T" Mnemonic for the Collegial Model:** > - **P** = Partnership > - **S** = Shared Authority > - **T** = Trust (Mutual) > > If you write these three words prominently in your answer and explain each with one example, you have covered the *core* of the topic and will secure the bulk of the marks.

topics.title: Human Value Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT803 - ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOUR AND BUSINESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human Value Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Value Model (HVM)**: A comprehensive conceptual framework that defines a human being as a co-existence of the **Self ("I" / Consciousness)** and the **Body (Material Self)**, and describes a continuous, cyclic process of **Need → Program → Activity → Fulfilment** through which the individual pursues **happiness, peace, and satisfaction** while simultaneously nurturing a set of universal, time-tested **human values** (such as Trust, Respect, Affection, Care, Guidance, Reverence, Glory, Gratitude, and Love) that govern the quality of interactions with other human beings.

The model is foundational in the **Value Education framework** adopted in KTU's professional ethics and behavioural science curriculum, providing the philosophical basis for understanding motivation, interpersonal behaviour, and ethical decision-making in organizations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Driver and the Vehicle"**

Imagine a human being as a **car with a driver**:

- The **Driver** is the **"I"** — the conscious self, the inner knower, the source of awareness, will, and decision-making.
- The **Vehicle** is the **Body** — the physical instrument (chassis, engine, wheels) that the driver uses to travel through life.
- The **Fuel** is the **Human Values** — without the right fuel (values), neither the engine runs smoothly, nor does the journey become meaningful.
- The **Roadmap (Need)** is the desire for **Happiness** — the driver (I) has a continuous need (destination) and the body moves toward fulfilling it.
- The **Cyclic Journey (Need → Programme → Activity → Fulfilment)** is the trip — once one destination is reached, a new need emerges, and the cycle restarts.

Just as a driver who ignores traffic rules (values) reaches neither safely nor happily, a human being who acts only on bodily needs without nurturing the self (I) and values will experience a sense of incompleteness, dissatisfaction, and conflict — exactly what the **Human Value Model** explains.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Self ("I")** — the conscious, dimensionless entity that is the source of all awareness and intent.
- **Body** — the material, physical, and perceptible entity of the human being.
- **Happiness (Ananda)** — the ultimate, continuous need of the Self.
- **Need** — a state of conscious or material deficiency that prompts activity.
- **Programme** — the plan/decision formulated by the intellect to fulfil the need.
- **Fulfilment (Evaluation)** — the outcome assessment that either satisfies or triggers a new need.
- **Human Values** — 9 core values (Trust, Respect, Affection, Care, Guidance, Reverence, Glory, Gratitude, Love).
- **Four Levels of Existence** — Body, Mind, Intellect, Self.

> [!NOTE]
> The Human Value Model is a **diagnostic and developmental tool** — it does not have numerical units; rather, it operates on **conceptual dimensions** (Self vs. Body) and **qualitative states** (satisfaction/dissatisfaction).

### 1.4 Why the Human Value Model Matters in Organizational Behaviour

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the Human Value Model provides the **philosophical root** for understanding:
- **Motivation Theories** (Maslow, Herzberg) — explaining the *why* behind human striving.
- **Attitudes and Values at Work** — influencing job satisfaction, OCB (Organizational Citizenship Behaviour), and ethics.
- **Leadership & Team Behaviour** — value-based leadership styles.
- **Conflict & Negotiation** — value-clash and value-alignment dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Dimensions of the Human Being

The Human Value Model postulates that a human being is a **co-existence of two distinct yet inseparable entities**:

| Dimension | Nature | Primary Need | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self ("I")** | Conscious, non-material, unchanging, the *knower* | **Happiness, Peace, Bliss (continuous need)** | Satisfaction, Fulfillment |
| **Body** | Material, physical, changeable, the *instrument* | **Physical facilities (temporary, cyclic need)** | Comfort, Sustenance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Self's need for happiness is **continuous and qualitative** (it cannot be "stockpiled"), while the body's need is **temporary and quantitative** (it can be fulfilled repeatedly — food, water, shelter). This asymmetry is the source of all human striving.

### 2.2 The Four Levels (Domains) of Human Existence

| Level | Function | Output (Material/Immaterial) | Example in OB |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Body** | Physical action, sensation | Material | Performing a task, attendance |
| **Mind** | Storage of thoughts, feelings | Information (Immaterial) | Attitudes, perceptions, motivation |
| **Intellect** | Analysis, decision, discrimination | Understanding (Immaterial) | Decision-making, problem-solving |
| **Self ("I")** | Realization, witnessing, directing | Realization (Immaterial) | Purpose, ethics, value-internalization |

The model emphasizes that the **Self (I) is the master**, and Body, Mind, and Intellect are its **subordinates**. When the intellect is properly aligned with the Self, decisions become value-driven rather than desire-driven.

### 2.3 The Cyclic Process of Human Aspiration

The Human Value Model operates as a **continuous cycle**, often depicted as the **Wheel of Human Aspiration**:

1. **Need (Avastha)** — Recognition of a deficiency (e.g., hunger, loneliness, or a search for meaning).
2. **Programme (Vichar)** — The intellect plans, evaluates alternatives, and decides on a course of action.
3. **Activity (Karma)** — The body executes the programme; the mind supports with thoughts/feelings.
4. **Fulfilment (Priti)** — Evaluation of outcome; if the need is satisfied, the cycle moves to the next need.

> [!NOTE]
> This cycle is **inseparable from values**. The *quality* of the cycle depends on the *values* with which the intellect programmes the activity. Without values, the cycle produces physical comfort but inner dissatisfaction; with values, it produces *both* material progress and inner fulfilment.

### 2.4 The Nine Universal Human Values

| # | Value | Definition | Workplace Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trust (Vishwas)** | The foundational basis of all relationships | Trust in leadership, team cohesion |
| 2 | **Respect (Sammaan)** | Acknowledging the inherent worth of every human | Respectful communication, dignity at work |
| 3 | **Affection (Sneha)** | A close, warm, caring feeling | Team bonding, mentor-mentee warmth |
| 4 | **Care (Mamta)** | Feeling of belonging and protection | Employee welfare, work-life balance |
| 5 | **Guidance (Vatsalya)** | Nurturing the other toward self-fulfilment | Coaching, mentoring, training |
| 6 | **Reverence (Shraddha)** | Honoring the other for their excellence or role | Recognition, respect for expertise |
| 7 | **Glory (Gaurav)** | Acknowledging excellence and contribution | Awards, appreciation, status |
| 8 | **Gratitude (Kritagyata)** | Thankfulness for what one has received | Acknowledging others' contributions |
| 9 | **Love (Prema)** | The highest, unconditional affection | Inclusive leadership, psychological safety |

### 2.5 The Five-Dimensional Framework of Value Realization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a value to be *enriched* in a human being, it must pass through five sequential steps:

| Step | Dimension | Meaning | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conception (Sankalpa)** | The vision or idea of the value | Clarity of purpose |
| 2 | **Confirmation (Vivechana)** | Affirmation by analysis | Conviction |
| 3 | **Conviction (Astitva)** | Deep inner belief | Willingness |
| 4 | **Cultivation (Abhyasa)** | Continuous practice | Habituation |
| 5 | **Character (Vyaktitva)** | Value becomes part of one's personality | Natural expression |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of the Human Value Model in Engineering & Management

- **HRM**: Designing value-based hiring, performance appraisal, and organizational culture.
- **Leadership Development**: Building ethical, value-driven leaders.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Diagnosing value-misalignment as a root cause.
- **Customer Relations**: Building trust and respect as competitive advantages.
- **Engineering Ethics**: Linking technical decisions to broader human welfare.
- **Self-Management**: For engineers and managers, the model provides a personal framework for **goal setting, decision-making, and stress reduction** by aligning bodily activity with the deeper needs of the Self.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Logical Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Human Value Model: A Logical Walkthrough

> The Human Value Model is derived by observing the **fundamental nature of human striving** and breaking it into its logically irreducible components. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

---

**Step 1 — Observe that every human activity has a starting point.**

Every action, decision, or behaviour begins with a **felt deficiency** — a state where something is needed.

$$\text{Trigger State} = \{\text{Physical deficiency} \} \cup \{\text{Psychological/Inner deficiency}\}$$

> Explanation: Hunger is a physical deficiency; loneliness is an inner deficiency. Both are real and both motivate action.

---

**Step 2 — Classify the deficiency into Self-need or Body-need.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Needs} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Self-Needs (I):} & \{ \text{Happiness, Peace, Satisfaction, Bliss, Harmony} \} \\ \text{Body-Needs:} & \{ \text{Food, Water, Shelter, Comfort, Rest, Safety} \} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: The model's foundational claim is that **the Self's needs are continuous** (a satisfied self-need does not create a new one; rather, the need is *enriched*), while **the body's needs are cyclic** (a satisfied bodily need gives rise to the next need — e.g., after eating, hunger returns).

---

**Step 3 — Recognize the need requires processing before action.**

A raw need (e.g., anger) cannot be acted upon without **evaluation**. This evaluation is performed by the **Intellect**, with support from **Memory** (stored in the Mind).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Need} \xrightarrow[\text{Memory (Mind)}]{\text{Input to}} \text{Intellect} \xrightarrow{\text{Analysis}} \text{Programme}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: The mind provides data (memories, feelings, attitudes), and the intellect analyses them and formulates a **programme of action**.

---

**Step 4 — Execute the programme through the Body.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Programme} \xrightarrow{\text{Order from "I"}} \text{Activity} \xrightarrow{\text{Body executes}} \text{Action Output}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: The Self ("I") is the final authorizing authority. The activity is what the body does, but it is the "I" that has sanctioned it.

---

**Step 5 — Evaluate the outcome against the original need (Fulfilment).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Activity Outcome} \xrightarrow{\text{Comparison}} \begin{cases} \text{Satisfied} \Rightarrow \text{Cycle pauses / new need emerges} \\ \text{Unsatisfied} \Rightarrow \text{Re-plan via Intellect} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: If the outcome satisfies the need, the cycle is complete (or moves to the next need). If not, the intellect re-programmes — this is the **iterative, learning aspect** of human behaviour.

---

**Step 6 — Introduce Values as the *quality filter* of the cycle.**

This is the central insight of the Human Value Model — **values are not added externally; they are the *basis of evaluation* of the activity's outcome**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Programme Quality} = f(\text{Values internalised by the "I"})
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: Two people with the *same need* (e.g., promotion) can produce *different programmes* — one is ethical, the other is unethical. The difference is the **value-frame** internalised by their Self.

---

**Step 7 — Complete the Cyclic Model.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Need} \longrightarrow \text{Programme (via Intellect)} \longrightarrow \text{Activity (via Body)} \longrightarrow \text{Fulfilment} \longrightarrow \text{Next Need}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: The cycle is **continuous and indivisible**. Stopping it (i.e., suppressing a need without resolving it) is the root cause of psychological stress and behavioural disorders.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Self vs. Body — A Comparative View

| Parameter | Self ("I") | Body |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Conscious, subtle | Material, gross |
| **Dimensions** | Dimensionless | Three-dimensional + time |
| **Visibility** | Invisible, felt through awareness | Visible, tangible |
| **Need Type** | Continuous (Happiness) | Cyclic (Material facilities) |
| **Need Outcome** | Enrichment (qualitative) | Satisfaction (quantitative) |
| **Knowledge Source** | Intuition, self-realization | Perception, senses |
| **Function in OB** | Sets purpose, values | Executes tasks, produces output |
| **Harmful if** | Ignored → Meaning crisis | Indulged excessively → Health/ethical issues |
| **Substance** | Pure consciousness | Panchabhutas (5 elements) |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mind vs. Intellect — A Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Mind (Manas) | Intellect (Buddhi) |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Generates thoughts, stores feelings | Analyzes, discriminates, decides |
| **Mode of Operation** | Spontaneous, reactive | Deliberate, reflective |
| **Content** | Desires, doubts, emotions, memories | Logic, judgment, values |
| **Dependency** | Depends on inputs from senses & "I" | Depends on inputs from "I" and Mind |
| **Role in Decision** | Provides the *raw data* | Provides the *processed decision* |
| **Example** | "I am angry at my boss" (thought) | "Anger is harmful to my long-term interest; I will respond calmly" (decision) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight**: The most frequently-asked conceptual distinction in KTU exams is between **Mind** and **Intellect**. Most students mistakenly treat them as synonymous. The Human Value Model draws a clear line: *Mind* is the seat of thoughts and feelings; *Intellect* is the seat of analysis and decision-making.

### 3.4 The Value-Enrichment Equation (Conceptual)

While the Human Value Model is qualitative, we can represent value enrichment as a function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_e = V_o \times (1 + r)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $V_e$ = the *enriched* value state of the human being
- $V_o$ = the *original* value content
- $r$ = the rate of *self-effort* (practice, reflection)
- $n$ = the number of *cycles* of practice (habituation)

> Explanation: This is an **analogy**, not a literal equation. It conveys the idea that *values are not "taught once and forgotten"* — they are *enriched* through continuous practice, cycle after cycle, like compound interest.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Applying the HVM to an Organizational Scenario

**Scenario**: A project manager (Riya) discovers her team is under-reporting defects to meet a quarterly deadline.

**Step-by-Step Application of the Human Value Model**:

1. **Need (Recognition by Riya)**: The manager perceives a deficiency — a gap between *as-is performance* and *should-be performance* (quality drop).
2. **Programme (via Intellect)**: She analyses whether the cause is *systemic* (workload, tools) or *behavioural* (values like Trust, Honesty missing in the team).
3. **Activity (via Body)**: She conducts a transparent review meeting, not to punish but to *restore* the value-frame of the team.
4. **Fulfilment (Evaluation)**: The team re-aligns with quality; defect reporting improves; Riya experiences satisfaction (Self-need) and improved product quality (Body-need).
5. **Value-Frame Used**: **Trust, Respect, Guidance, Gratitude** — these are the 4 of the 9 universal values most relevant here.
6. **Outcome**: The *next need* emerges — sustaining the culture, leading to the next cycle.

> This is exactly the type of *application-based question* KTU examiners use to test understanding of the HVM beyond rote definitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1: The Co-Existence of Self and Body (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HUM_BEING["HUMAN BEING COEXISTENCE MODEL"]
        subgraph SELF_DOMAIN["SELF DOMAIN I"]
            SELFCON["CONSCIOUSNESS I"]
            SELFFEEL["HAPPINESS NEED"]
            SELFOUT["PEACE BLISS SATISFACTION"]
        end
        subgraph BODY_DOMAIN["BODY DOMAIN"]
            BODYCON["PHYSICAL BODY"]
            BODYNEED["MATERIAL NEEDS"]
            BODYOUT["FOOD SHELTER COMFORT"]
        end
        SELFCON --- BODYCON
        SELFFEEL --- BODYNEED
        SELFOUT --- BODYOUT
    end
    SELFCON -. AWARENESS DIRECTS .-> BODYCON
    BODYCON -. ACTION REALIZES .-> SELFCON
```

> **Reading Guide**: The top two blocks represent the **Self ("I")** — the conscious, immaterial dimension. The bottom two blocks represent the **Body** — the material, action-performing dimension. The solid horizontal lines show the *parallel coexistence*; the dashed arrows show the *mutual interaction*: the Self directs the Body, and the Body's actions realize the Self's intentions.

---

### 4.2 Figure 2: The Cyclic Process of Human Aspiration (Wheel of Fulfillment)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["NEED AVASTHA"] --> B["PROGRAMME VICHAR"]
    B --> C["ACTIVITY KARMA"]
    C --> D["FULFILMENT PRITI"]
    D -->|"New need emerges"| A
    A -. FEEDS .-> E["INTELLECT ANALYSIS"]
    E -. FORMULATES .-> B
    B -. ORDERS .-> F["BODY EXECUTION"]
    F -. EXECUTES .-> C
    C -. OUTPUT .-> D
    D -. SATISFACTION .-> G["VALUES TRUST RESPECT LOVE"]
    G -. QUALITY FILTER .-> B
```

> **Reading Guide**: Read this diagram clockwise. The cycle is **Need → Programme → Activity → Fulfilment → Next Need**. The **Values block** on the right is *not* an extra step — it is the **quality filter** that determines whether the Programme is value-aligned. The Intellect and Body are the supporting sub-systems.

---

### 4.3 Figure 3: The Four Domains of Human Existence (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FOURLEVELS["FOUR DOMAINS OF HUMAN EXISTENCE"]
        LV1["LEVEL 4 SELF I  CONSCIOUSNESS REALIZATION"]
        LV2["LEVEL 3 INTELLECT BUDDHI  DECISION DISCRIMINATION"]
        LV3["LEVEL 2 MIND MANAS  THOUGHTS FEELINGS MEMORY"]
        LV4["LEVEL 1 BODY SHAREER  PHYSICAL ACTION SENSATION"]
    end
    LV1 -->|"DIRECTS"| LV2
    LV2 -->|"ANALYSES INPUT FROM"| LV3
    LV3 -->|"STORES PROCESSES INPUT FROM"| LV4
    LV4 -->|"FEEDS SENSORY DATA TO"| LV3
    LV3 -->|"PROVIDES RAW DATA TO"| LV2
    LV2 -->|"EXECUTES DECISIONS VIA"| LV4
```

> **Reading Guide**: The diagram shows a **four-level hierarchy**. The Self (top) is the master; it directs the Intellect. The Intellect uses the Mind's data and orders the Body to act. The Body's actions are converted to sensory data that returns to the Mind, completing the loop. This is the classic **Self → Intellect → Mind → Body** flow of the HVM.

---

### 4.4 Figure 4: The Nine Universal Human Values — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph COREVALS["THE NINE UNIVERSAL HUMAN VALUES"]
        V1["VALUE 1 TRUST VISHWAS"]
        V2["VALUE 2 RESPECT SAMMAAN"]
        V3["VALUE 3 AFFECTION SNEHA"]
        V4["VALUE 4 CARE MAMTA"]
        V5["VALUE 5 GUIDANCE VATSALYA"]
        V6["VALUE 6 REVERENCE SHRADDHA"]
        V7["VALUE 7 GLORY GAURAV"]
        V8["VALUE 8 GRATITUDE KRITAGYATA"]
        V9["VALUE 9 LOVE PREMA"]
    end
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> V4
    V4 --> V5
    V5 --> V6
    V6 --> V7
    V7 --> V8
    V8 --> V9
    V9 -->|"REALIZATION"| V1
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V1
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V2
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V3
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V4
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V5
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V6
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V7
    V9 -. ENCOMPASSES ALL .-> V8
```

> **Reading Guide**: The diagram shows a **pyramidal relationship** among the nine values. **Love** is the most comprehensive, encompassing all others. **Trust** is the foundation — without trust, no other value can stand. The arrows show the *progression* and *enrichment* relationship: each value leads naturally to the next higher value.

---

### 4.5 Figure 5: Value Enrichment — The Five-Step Realization Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["STEP 1 CONCEPTION SANKALPA  Vision of the value"] --> S2["STEP 2 CONFIRMATION VIVE  Analysis and affirmation"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 CONVICTION ASTITVA  Deep inner belief"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 CULTIVATION ABHYASA  Continuous practice"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 CHARACTER VYAKTITVA  Natural expression of value"]
    S5 -. ENRICHED HUMAN .-> S1
```

> **Reading Guide**: This is the **process by which a value moves from being a "concept" to becoming one's "character"**. It is a *non-linear, iterative* process — even after a value is "enriched" into character, new levels of the same value keep emerging (the cycle restarts at a higher level).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, UEHUT803 Module 1]**

**Q1. Define the Human Value Model. List its three core components.**
*(3 Marks — CO1, Cognitive Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The Human Value Model is a conceptual framework that describes a human being as a co-existence of the **Self ("I")** and the **Body**, operating through a continuous cyclic process of **Need → Programme → Activity → Fulfilment**, with **human values** serving as the quality filter of the entire cycle.
- **Three core components (1 mark):**
  1. **The Two Dimensions** — Self ("I") and Body.
  2. **The Cyclic Process** — Need → Programme → Activity → Fulfilment.
  3. **The Nine Universal Human Values** — Trust, Respect, Affection, Care, Guidance, Reverence, Glory, Gratitude, Love.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between the Mind and the Intellect as conceptualized in the Human Value Model. Why is the distinction important in organizational behaviour?**
*(3 Marks — CO1, Cognitive Level: Understand/Analyze)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Mind (1 mark):** The Mind is the seat of **thoughts, feelings, memories, and desires**. It generates raw, often reactive, mental content (e.g., "I am angry at my boss").
- **Intellect (1 mark):** The Intellect is the seat of **analysis, discrimination, judgment, and decision-making**. It processes the Mind's raw input and produces a *decision* (e.g., "Anger is harmful to my long-term interest; I will respond calmly").
- **Importance in OB (1 mark):** The distinction is crucial because **most workplace problems arise not from the lack of intellect, but from the dominance of the mind (reactions) over the intellect (reflection)**. A value-driven manager is one who lets the intellect, guided by values, override the mind's reactive impulses — leading to better decision quality, conflict resolution, and team trust.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, UEHUT803 Module 1, 14-Mark Module Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Human Value Model** as conceptualized in value education. Discuss the **co-existence of Self and Body** and the **cyclic process of Need–Programme–Activity–Fulfilment** with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **nine universal human values**. With the help of an organizational scenario (e.g., a manager handling a team crisis), explain how any **four** of these values are essential for effective leadership. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A**

**(a) The Human Value Model — Co-existence and the Cyclic Process (7 Marks)**

**1. Co-existence of Self and Body (3 marks):**
The Human Value Model begins with the fundamental proposition that a human being is **not just a body**. A human being is a co-existence of two distinct yet inseparable entities:

- **The Self ("I")** — the conscious, non-material, dimensionless entity that is the source of awareness, will, and decision-making. Its primary need is **continuous happiness, peace, and satisfaction**.
- **The Body** — the material, physical, three-dimensional entity that is the *instrument* of action. Its primary need is **physical facilities** (food, water, shelter, comfort), which are cyclic in nature.

**Example (1 mark):** When an employee works overtime, the **body** receives fatigue (a physical need) while the **self** may experience satisfaction (a self-need) — the two needs are simultaneously being met, or not, by the same activity. This explains why some "successful" professionals feel deeply unfulfilled — their self-need is unmet despite the body's needs being well-satisfied.

**2. The Cyclic Process of Need–Programme–Activity–Fulfilment (3 marks):**
The model describes human behaviour as a **continuous, four-stage cycle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Need} \rightarrow \text{Programme} \rightarrow \text{Activity} \rightarrow \text{Fulfilment} \rightarrow \text{Next Need}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Need (Avastha)**: A felt deficiency — physical (hunger, fatigue) or psychological (loneliness, lack of meaning).
- **Programme (Vichar)**: The intellect evaluates alternatives, considering the mind's data, and formulates a plan.
- **Activity (Karma)**: The body, under the "I"s direction, executes the programme.
- **Fulfilment (Priti)**: The outcome is evaluated; if satisfying, the cycle completes and a new need emerges.

**Example (1 mark):** A student feels the *need* to score well → designs a *programme* (study plan) → executes *activity* (studies) → experiences *fulfilment* (good score) → the next need (placement preparation) emerges — the cycle continues.

**Key Insight:** **Values are the quality filter** of this cycle. The same need, processed through different value-frames, produces different programmes. Two employees with the same need for "recognition" may produce one ethical programme and one unethical — depending on whether values like **Honesty, Trust, and Respect** are internalised.

---

**(b) The Nine Universal Human Values & Their Role in Leadership (7 Marks)**

**1. The Nine Universal Human Values (3 marks):**

The Human Value Model identifies **nine time-tested, universal human values** that govern the quality of all human interactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{ \text{Trust, Respect, Affection, Care, Guidance, Reverence, Glory, Gratitude, Love} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each value is **not a standalone skill** but an **expression of the relationship between the Self and others**. Trust is foundational; Love is the most comprehensive — encompassing all others.

**2. Organizational Scenario (Manager Handling a Team Crisis) (4 marks):**

**Scenario:** Ms. Ananya, a project manager, discovers that her team has been facing burnout due to unrealistic deadlines imposed by senior management. Two senior team members have resigned; morale is at an all-time low.

**Application of four values:**

| Value | Application in the Scenario |
|---|---|
| **Trust (Vishwas)** (1 mark) | Ananya must first **build trust** by being transparent with her team about the deadlines and the constraints she is under. She shares the full context — not just the orders — so the team sees her as an ally, not a tool. |
| **Respect (Sammaan)** (1 mark) | She **respects each team member's dignity** by acknowledging their efforts and listening to their grievances without dismissing them as complaints. |
| **Care (Mamta)** (1 mark) | She **genuinely cares** for her team's well-being — pushes back against the unrealistic deadlines to senior management, even at a personal cost to her own targets. |
| **Guidance (Vatsalya)** (1 mark) | She **guides** the team to reorganize work, prioritize tasks, and develop resilience — nurturing their long-term capability, not just short-term output. |

**Outcome:** The team, sensing the value-frame, regains trust in her leadership, recommits to the project, and senior management adjusts the deadlines based on her honest representation. This is **value-driven leadership** in action — and the Human Value Model provides its conceptual foundation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What are the **four levels (domains) of human existence** in the Human Value Model? Explain each with an example of how it operates in an organizational context. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **five-step process of value enrichment** (Conception → Confirmation → Conviction → Cultivation → Character). Why is value enrichment described as a *cyclic* process, and not a one-time event? *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B**

**(a) The Four Levels of Human Existence (7 Marks)**

The Human Value Model describes a human being as functioning at **four inter-related levels**, each with a distinct function and output.

| Level | Function | Output | OB Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Body** (2 marks) | Physical action, sensation, movement | Material work output | Completing a coding task, attending a meeting |
| **Mind** (2 marks) | Generates & stores thoughts, feelings, memories | Information, attitudes | An employee's positive attitude toward the organization |
| **Intellect** (2 marks) | Analyzes, discriminates, decides, evaluates | Understanding, judgment | A manager choosing between two strategies using rational analysis |
| **Self ("I")** (1 mark) | Realizes, witnesses, directs, values | Realization, purpose, character | A leader recognizing that her *purpose* is to develop her team, not just deliver numbers |

**Key Insight:** The four levels are **not parallel** — they form a **hierarchy** in which the **Self is the master**, the **Intellect is the decision-maker**, the **Mind is the data store**, and the **Body is the executor**. When this hierarchy is respected (Self → Intellect → Mind → Body), decisions are value-driven; when inverted (Body → Mind → Intellect → Self being ignored), decisions become pleasure-driven, often leading to ethical lapses and burnout.

---

**(b) The Five-Step Process of Value Enrichment (7 Marks)**

**1. The Five Steps (5 marks):**

The Human Value Model describes how a value is *enriched* (internalized) in a human being through a **five-step sequential process**:

| Step | Name | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conception (Sankalpa)** (1 mark) | The initial idea/vision of the value | A new manager *conceives* the value of "Trust" by reading about it |
| 2 | **Confirmation (Vivechana)** (1 mark) | Affirmation by analysis — "Yes, this value is correct" | She analyses the consequences of trust vs. distrust in teams |
| 3 | **Conviction (Astitva)** (1 mark) | Deep inner belief — the value becomes a *personal* truth | She now *believes* that trust is essential, not just theoretically |
| 4 | **Cultivation (Abhyasa)** (1 mark) | Continuous practice in real situations | She practices transparency in every team meeting, every communication |
| 5 | **Character (Vyaktitva)** (1 mark) | The value becomes her natural expression | She is *known* as a trusting, transparent leader — the value is now her character |

**2. Why the Process is Cyclic, Not One-Time (2 marks):**

Value enrichment is **cyclic** because:
- Even after a value reaches the "Character" stage, **new, deeper levels of the same value keep emerging** — e.g., a person who has *character-level* Trust can still discover deeper forms of Trust (e.g., trust in self, trust in the universe). The cycle restarts at a *higher level*.
- **Values decay without practice** — cultivation is a continuous need, not a one-time act. If a manager who has reached the "Character" stage of Trust stops practising it, the value weakens (a phenomenon analogous to a muscle atrophying without use).
- **Human beings are dynamic**; new contexts (cultural, organizational, personal) demand renewed *conception → confirmation → conviction* for the same value.

Hence, the enrichment of a value is a **lifelong, cyclic journey**, not a destination.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on the Human Value Model:**

1. **Confusing Mind with Intellect (–2 marks):** Most answers treat "mind" and "intellect" as synonyms. The KTU board specifically tests the distinction: *Mind = thoughts/feelings*; *Intellect = analysis/decision*. Always clarify both.

2. **Forgetting the "Cyclic" nature (–1 mark):** When explaining the cycle, students often stop at "Fulfilment" and forget to mention the **emergence of the next need** and the **continuity of the cycle**.

3. **Listing values without explaining their role in OB (–2 marks):** Simply writing "Trust, Respect, Affection..." gets partial credit. You must **apply** the value to an OB context (e.g., "Trust in leadership reduces employee turnover") to score full marks.

4. **Treating HVM as a religious/spiritual concept (–1 mark):** The HVM is a **secular, behavioural-science framework**. Avoid language that makes it sound purely religious. Use neutral, academic terminology ("consciousness," "realization," "value-frame") for full marks.

5. **Skipping examples (–1 mark):** KTU examiners explicitly look for **one real-world / organizational example per 3–4 marks**. Always include a short, relevant example.

6. **Ignoring the Body–Self co-existence (–1 mark):** Several students describe the cycle without explicitly stating that the human being is a **co-existence of Self and Body**, which is the *foundational* claim of the model. Always begin with this statement.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before the exam**

- [ ] **Human Value Model (HVM)** is a *conceptual framework* that defines a human being as the **co-existence of Self ("I") and Body**.
- [ ] The **Self's need** is *continuous happiness*; the **Body's need** is *cyclic material facilities*.
- [ ] The **HVM cycle** has four stages: **Need → Programme → Activity → Fulfilment**, then it restarts.
- [ ] **Values are the quality filter** of the cycle — they determine whether the programme is ethical or unethical, sustainable or destructive.
- [ ] **Four Levels of Existence**: Body (action), Mind (thoughts/feelings), Intellect (decision), Self (realization/direction).
- [ ] The **Self is the master**; Intellect is the *analyst*; Mind is the *store*; Body is the *executor*. Misuse of this hierarchy = unethical decisions.
- [ ] **Nine Universal Human Values**: Trust, Respect, Affection, Care, Guidance, Reverence, Glory, Gratitude, Love. **Trust is foundational; Love is all-encompassing.**
- [ ] **Value Enrichment** is a *five-step cyclic process*: Conception → Confirmation → Conviction → Cultivation → Character.
- [ ] Value enrichment is **lifelong** — values decay without practice; new levels keep emerging.
- [ ] The HVM is **secular, behaviourally relevant, and applicable to OB** — not a religious doctrine.
- [ ] In **Kerala's KTU 2024 scheme**, this topic is the **philosophical foundation** for understanding motivation, leadership, ethics, and conflict resolution in Module 1.
- [ ] Always **differentiate Mind vs. Intellect** in any answer — it is a frequently-tested distinction.
- [ ] Always **end with an organizational example** — it is the KTU board's "differentiator" between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer.

---

> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES — Human Value Model | UEHUT803 Module 1**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT803 - ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOUR AND BUSINESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Individual Behaviour – Attitudes and Job Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Attitude – The Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Greenberg & Baron, OB Textbook Standard)**
> An **Attitude** is a learned predisposition to respond in a consistently favourable or unfavourable manner with respect to a given object, person, event, or idea. It is a psychological tendency expressed by evaluating a particular entity with some degree of favour or disfavour.

In the KTU 2024 management syllabus, attitudes are studied as **evaluative statements** (either positive or negative) about objects, people, or events that reflect how an individual feels about something. Attitudes are not behaviours themselves — they are the *internal readiness* that precedes behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Attitudes Matter in Engineering Management (KTU Context):**
> As future engineers leading projects, B.Tech graduates must understand that employee attitudes directly influence team cohesion, project success, innovation willingness, and safety compliance on industrial sites.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Attitude as the "Inner Lens"

Imagine a pair of **tinted sunglasses** that you wear every day.

- The **tint** (colour) of the lens is your *attitude* — it does not change the actual world outside, but it filters how you *perceive* and *respond* to it.
- A **rose-tinted lens** (positive attitude) makes the same office look brighter and tasks seem more meaningful.
- A **grey-tinted lens** (negative attitude) makes the same office feel dull and work feel burdensome.
- The world (organisation) is the *same* — but the **lens** changes the experience, the energy, and the behaviour.

**Key takeaway:** Just like a lens, attitudes are **internal, learned, and influence how we act**, even when external reality is unchanged.

---

## 1.3 Job Satisfaction — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Locke's Classic Definition)**
> **Job Satisfaction** is a pleasurable or positive emotional state resulting from the appraisal of one's job or job experiences. It represents an affective (feeling) response to a job, based on comparing desired outcomes with actual outcomes.

Edwin A. Locke's foundational definition (1976) — the most cited definition in KTU/Humanities OB modules — emphasises that job satisfaction is the **result of a mental evaluation** between *what one wants from a job* and *what one perceives the job as providing*.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Job Satisfaction as a "Thermometer Reading"

Think of **Job Satisfaction as a thermometer inside an employee's mind**.

- **What the thermometer measures:** how "warm" (satisfied) or "cold" (dissatisfied) an employee feels about their work.
- **What raises the temperature (satisfaction):** fair pay, recognition, growth, supportive supervisor, meaningful work.
- **What lowers the temperature (dissatisfaction):** toxic culture, no growth, low pay, bad manager, poor work conditions.
- **The thermometer does not change the weather (work itself)** — it only *measures* the emotional climate inside the person.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Insight:** Job satisfaction is **subjective**. Two engineers with identical jobs, identical pay, and identical managers can have *vastly different* job satisfaction levels because of differing personal expectations and value systems.

---

## 1.5 The Three Core Building Blocks You Must Know

| Concept | What It Is | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Attitude** | A learned, evaluative predisposition to act | Predisposition |
| **Job Satisfaction** | A positive emotional state from job appraisal | Affective Response |
| **Job Involvement** | Degree to which a person identifies psychologically with their job | Psychological Identification |
| **Organisational Commitment** | Emotional attachment to and identification with the organisation | Loyalty/Bond |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2-Mark/3-Mark Questions:** Always remember the trio — *Attitude → Job Satisfaction → Organisational Commitment*. Examiners love asking students to distinguish between these three closely related constructs.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ABC Model of Attitude (Core KTU Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ABC Tri-component Model** is the most heavily tested attitude framework in KTU exams. Every component must be memorised with a one-line definition and example.

The **ABC Model** breaks every attitude into **three interlinked components**:

### **A — Affective Component (Feelings)**
- Refers to the **emotional** segment of an attitude.
- It is *how a person feels* about the object.
- **KTU Example:** "I *love* my work" / "I *hate* Monday mornings."

### **B — Behavioural Component (Intent to Act)**
- Refers to the **way** we *intend to behave* or actually behave toward the object.
- It is the predisposition to act in a certain way.
- **KTU Example:** "I will *quit* this job" / "I will *volunteer* for the project."

### **C — Cognitive Component (Beliefs/Knowledge)**
- Refers to the **beliefs, opinions, knowledge, or information** a person holds about the object.
- It is the thinking/evaluative part.
- **KTU Example:** "My manager is *incompetent*" / "This company pays *fair wages*."

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 7-Mark Questions:** When asked to "Explain the components of attitude with examples," always draw a triangle with A, B, C and give ONE clear workplace example for each component.

---

## 2.2 Types of Attitudes in Organisational Behaviour

### **A. Job Satisfaction**
- A positive or negative evaluative judgement about one's job.
- Discussed in detail in Section 2.5.

### **B. Job Involvement**
- The **degree to which a person identifies psychologically** with their job.
- High job involvement = the job is a *central part* of one's self-concept and identity.
- **KTU Example:** A passionate mechanical engineer at ISRO who emotionally invests in satellite projects.

### **C. Organisational Commitment**
- The **emotional attachment** an employee feels toward their organisation.
- **Allen & Meyer (1991)** Three-Component Model is the KTU gold standard:
  1. **Affective Commitment** — Emotional attachment ("I *want* to stay").
  2. **Continuance Commitment** — Perceived cost of leaving ("I *need* to stay").
  3. **Normative Commitment** — Moral obligation ("I *ought* to stay").

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Affective commitment is the *strongest* predictor of retention. Normative and continuance are weaker but still relevant.

### **D. Perceived Organisational Support (POS)**
- The degree to which employees believe the organisation *values their contribution* and *cares about their well-being*.

### **E. Employee Engagement**
- A *higher-order construct* — the employee's *emotional and cognitive investment* in their work.
- Goes beyond satisfaction: engaged employees actively contribute to organisational success.

---

## 2.3 Attitude Formation — The "How" Behind Attitudes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 high-yield topic:** Examiners frequently ask "Explain the process of attitude formation."

Attitudes are formed (and changed) through **four primary mechanisms**:

### **1. Direct Experience (Direct Exposure)**
- The most powerful source of attitude formation.
- **KTU Example:** A first-year intern who is treated well by senior engineers develops a positive attitude toward the company.

### **2. Social Learning (Observational Learning)**
- Learning attitudes by **observing and imitating** others (parents, peers, role models, mentors).
- **KTU Example:** A junior engineer adopts a safety-first attitude after seeing her supervisor follow safety protocols religiously.

### **3. Conditioning (Classical & Operant)**
- **Classical Conditioning:** Repeated pairing of an object with a positive/negative stimulus creates an attitude.
- **Operant Conditioning:** Behaviours leading to rewards are repeated and develop into attitudes.
- **KTU Example:** Every time the team completes a project on time, the manager rewards them — the team develops a positive attitude toward deadlines.

### **4. Cognitive Dissonance (Festinger's Theory)**
- Discomfort caused by holding **two conflicting attitudes or behaviours** motivates attitude change.
- **KTU Example:** A person who smokes (behaviour) but believes smoking is harmful (attitude) experiences dissonance → either changes the attitude (rationalises) or changes the behaviour (quits).

---

## 2.4 Cognitive Dissonance Theory (Detailed KTU Treatment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Leon Festinger's Cognitive Dissonance Theory (1957)** is a compulsory KTU topic under Module 2.

**Core Premise:** Individuals seek **consistency** among their cognitions (beliefs, attitudes, behaviours). When inconsistency occurs (**dissonance**), the individual experiences psychological discomfort and is motivated to reduce it.

### **Three Ways to Reduce Dissonance:**

1. **Change the Behaviour** — e.g., stop smoking.
2. **Change the Cognition/Attitude** — e.g., convince yourself "smoking isn't really that harmful."
3. **Add a New Cognition** — e.g., "Yes, smoking is harmful, but it helps me cope with stress."

### **Real-World OB Application:**
- A software engineer believes in *work-life balance* (attitude) but is forced to do **80-hour weeks** (behaviour). The dissonance forces either:
  - Reduction in working hours (manager negotiation), OR
  - Internal rationalisation ("I am doing this for the project / startup dream"), OR
  - Adding new cognition ("This is a temporary crunch").

---

## 2.5 Job Satisfaction — Factors & Determinants

Job satisfaction is determined by a complex interaction of **personal, social, organisational, and environmental factors**. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these into the following high-yield categories:

### **A. Personal Factors**
- **Personality** (e.g., high-core self-evaluators tend to be more satisfied).
- **Age** (curvilinear relationship — U-shaped; dips in mid-career, rises near retirement).
- **Gender** (mixed evidence; in KTU OB, often no major differences).
- **Education Level** (higher education → often lower satisfaction due to unmet expectations).
- **Tenure** (longer tenure → sometimes higher, sometimes lower satisfaction).
- **Emotional Intelligence (EI)** (higher EI → better coping → higher satisfaction).

### **B. Organisational/Job-Related Factors**
- **Pay and Compensation** (hygiene factor in Herzberg's theory).
- **Work Itself** (variety, challenge, identity, autonomy, feedback — Job Characteristics Model).
- **Promotion Opportunities** (career growth).
- **Supervision Quality** (supportive, considerate leaders raise satisfaction).
- **Co-workers** (peer relations are a strong predictor of satisfaction).
- **Working Conditions** (physical environment, safety, ergonomics).
- **Work-Life Balance Policies** (flexible hours, remote work, parental leave).

### **C. Environmental/Extrinsic Factors**
- **Job Market Conditions** (alternative job availability).
- **Economic Conditions** (inflation, recession).
- **Industry Trends** (tech industry pay, manufacturing sector wages).

---

## 2.6 Outcomes of Job Satisfaction (Why It Matters)

| Outcome | Description | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | Positive (but moderate) correlation with job performance | Satisfied employees are slightly more productive |
| **Organisational Citizenship Behaviour (OCB)** | Satisfied employees engage in *discretionary helpful* behaviour | Helping a colleague without being asked |
| **Absenteeism** | Negative correlation | Dissatisfied employees take more sick leaves |
| **Turnover** | Strong negative correlation | Dissatisfied employees are more likely to quit |
| **Workplace Deviance** | Positive correlation with dissatisfaction | Sabotage, gossip, theft |
| **Customer Satisfaction** | Positive correlation | Happy employees → happy customers (service-profit chain) |
| **Life Satisfaction / Well-being** | Strong positive correlation | Job spills over into general life happiness |

---

## 2.7 The Job Satisfaction – Performance Debate (KTU 2024 Hot Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Does job satisfaction lead to performance, or does performance lead to satisfaction?"

### **The Traditional View (Hawthorne Studies Influence):**
- A **satisfied worker is a productive worker** (Human Relations approach).

### **The Modern View (Reverse Causation):**
- A **productive worker is a satisfied worker** — because high performance leads to rewards, which lead to satisfaction.

### **The Current Consensus (I/O Psychology Research):**
- The relationship is **moderate and complex**.
- Performance leads to satisfaction **more strongly** than satisfaction leads to performance.
- Both are likely influenced by **rewards and individual differences**.

> [!TIP]
> **For 7-Mark Questions:** Quote the I/O research saying "performance → satisfaction" is the stronger causal direction, but mention that both have a reciprocal relationship.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises the **key theoretical equations, models, and evaluative frameworks** that the KTU 2024 Board Examiner expects you to reproduce.

| # | Model / Concept | Core Equation or Relationship | Boundary Conditions | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ABC Model of Attitude** | Attitude = Affective + Behavioural + Cognitive | All 3 must be consistent | Predicting employee behaviour |
| 2 | **Cognitive Dissonance** | Dissonance = $\vert \text{Belief} - \text{Behaviour} \vert$ | Inconsistency > threshold | Explaining attitude change |
| 3 | **Locke's Job Satisfaction** | Satisfaction = $\text{Perceived Outcomes} - \text{Desired Outcomes}$ | Perceived $\geq$ Desired | Measuring satisfaction level |
| 4 | **Equity Theory (Adams)** | $\frac{O_{\text{self}}}{I_{\text{self}}} = \frac{O_{\text{other}}}{I_{\text{other}}}$ | Inequity causes tension | Pay fairness perception |
| 5 | **Expectancy Theory (Vroom)** | Motivation = Valence $\times$ Expectancy $\times$ Instrumentality | All 3 must be > 0 | Linking satisfaction to effort |
| 6 | **Herzberg Two-Factor** | Satisfaction = Motivators - Hygiene deficiencies | Hygiene absence → dissatisfaction | Job design/redesign |
| 7 | **Job Characteristics Model** | Skill Variety + Task Identity + Task Significance + Autonomy + Feedback $\rightarrow$ Satisfaction | High WPCS → high satisfaction | Work redesign |
| 8 | **Allen & Meyer Commitment** | Commitment = Affective + Continuance + Normative | Affective is the strongest | Retention strategies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values in formulas use $\vert ... \vert$ (LaTeX) instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Frameworks

| Framework | Engineering Workplace Use |
|---|---|
| **ABC Model** | Diagnose why a project team has low morale |
| **Cognitive Dissonance** | Understand resistance to new safety protocols |
| **Job Satisfaction Models** | Reduce attrition in IT companies (Infosys, TCS) |
| **Herzberg Theory** | Design engineer-friendly job roles (autonomous work) |
| **Job Characteristics Model** | Restructure assembly line tasks to improve engagement |
| **Equity Theory** | Resolve inter-departmental pay disputes |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is an OB / Humanities module (no mathematical derivations in the engineering sense), the "derivations" below are **analytical frameworks** — step-by-step logical deconstruction of models that KTU examiners expect students to articulate in Part B (14-mark) answers.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The ABC Model of Attitude

**Starting Premise:** Every attitude has three components that interact.

### **Step 1 — Define the Attitude Object**
Let the *attitude object* be denoted as $X$. In an OB context, $X$ may be a *job, supervisor, organisation, or work policy*.

### **Step 2 — Identify the Affective Component ($A_x$)**
The **emotional response** the individual has toward $X$.

$$A_x = \text{Emotional evaluation of } X$$

Examples: "I feel happy at work" (positive $A_x$) / "I feel frustrated" (negative $A_x$).

### **Step 3 — Identify the Cognitive Component ($C_x$)**
The **beliefs, knowledge, opinions** the individual holds about $X$.

$$C_x = \text{Belief structure about } X$$

Examples: "My job is secure" (positive $C_x$) / "My job is unstable" (negative $C_x$).

### **Step 4 — Identify the Behavioural Component ($B_x$)**
The **predisposition to act** toward $X$.

$$B_x = \text{Intent to act on } X$$

Examples: "I will stay with this company" / "I will quit."

### **Step 5 — Integrate the Components**
A complete attitude is the **sum of the three** interacting components:

$$\text{Attitude}_x = f(A_x, C_x, B_x)$$

For a **consistent attitude**, the three components must be **aligned**.

$$\text{Consistency} = \begin{cases} \text{Positive} & \text{if } \text{sign}(A_x) = \text{sign}(C_x) = \text{sign}(B_x) \\ \text{Negative (Dissonance)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### **Step 6 — Apply the Framework**
When the three components are inconsistent, the individual experiences **dissonance** (as per Festinger's theory) and is motivated to resolve it. This is the engine of **attitude change**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Locke's Job Satisfaction Equation

**Starting Premise:** Job satisfaction arises from a **mental comparison** of what one wants and what one gets.

### **Step 1 — Define Desired Outcomes**
Let $D$ = what the employee *wants* from the job (e.g., expected pay, growth, respect).

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i$$

where $d_i$ = the importance-weighted value of the $i$-th desired outcome.

### **Step 2 — Define Perceived Outcomes**
Let $P$ = what the employee *perceives* they are receiving.

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

where $p_i$ = the importance-weighted value of the $i$-th *perceived* outcome.

### **Step 3 — Compute the Gap**
The satisfaction level is a function of the gap:

$$S = P - D$$

### **Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

$$\text{Job Satisfaction} = \begin{cases} \text{High} & \text{if } P \gg D \text{ (Perceived > Desired)} \\ \text{Moderate} & \text{if } P \approx D \text{ (Equilibrium)} \\ \text{Low} & \text{if } P \ll D \text{ (Perceived < Desired)} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** An engineer who desires a salary of ₹12 LPA but earns ₹6 LPA will have *low job satisfaction*, regardless of the objective "fairness" of the salary.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Equity Theory (Adams)

**Starting Premise:** Employees compare their **outcome-to-input ratio** with that of referent others.

### **Step 1 — Define the Self Ratio**
For the focal employee ($S$):

$$R_{\text{self}} = \frac{O_{\text{self}}}{I_{\text{self}}}$$

where:
- $O_{\text{self}}$ = outcomes (pay, recognition, status) the focal employee receives
- $I_{\text{self}}$ = inputs (effort, skill, experience) the focal employee contributes

### **Step 2 — Define the Referent Comparison Ratio**
For the comparison person ($O$ — "Other"):

$$R_{\text{other}} = \frac{O_{\text{other}}}{I_{\text{other}}}$$

### **Step 3 — Equity Condition**
Equity exists when:

$$R_{\text{self}} = R_{\text{other}}$$

### **Step 4 — Inequity Outcomes**

$$\text{Equity Status} = \begin{cases} \text{Equitable} & \text{if } R_{\text{self}} = R_{\text{other}} \\ \text{Under-reward Inequity} & \text{if } R_{\text{self}} < R_{\text{other}} \\ \text{Over-reward Inequity} & \text{if } R_{\text{self}} > R_{\text{other}} \end{cases}$$

### **Step 5 — Reactions to Inequity**
1. **Change Inputs** — Reduce effort.
2. **Change Outcomes** — Ask for a raise.
3. **Change Referent** — Compare with a different colleague.
4. **Distort Perceptions** — Convince self the comparison is invalid.
5. **Leave the Situation** — Quit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners love Equity Theory because it predicts *concrete behaviour*. Always quote one numerical example.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory

**Starting Premise:** Job satisfaction and dissatisfaction are **NOT opposites** of the same continuum — they are two separate dimensions driven by different factors.

### **Step 1 — Two Independent Dimensions**
Let:
- $M$ = Motivator factors (e.g., achievement, recognition, growth, work itself)
- $H$ = Hygiene factors (e.g., pay, policies, supervision, working conditions)

### **Step 2 — Two Independent Axes**

$$\text{Satisfaction} \propto M$$

$$\text{Dissatisfaction} \propto \frac{1}{H_{\text{adequate}}}$$

In words:
- **High $M$** → **Satisfaction**.
- **Low $M$** → **No Satisfaction (but not dissatisfaction)**.
- **Adequate $H$** → **No Dissatisfaction (but no satisfaction either)**.
- **Inadequate $H$** → **Dissatisfaction**.

### **Step 3 — The 2D Matrix**

| | High Hygiene | Low Hygiene |
|---|---|---|
| **High Motivators** | **Ideal State** (Satisfied + No Dissatisfaction) | **Motivating but demotivating** (Satisfied but Dissatisfied) |
| **Low Motivators** | **Comfortable but unmotivated** (No Satisfaction + No Dissatisfaction) | **Worst State** (No Satisfaction + Dissatisfied) |

> [!TIP]
> **For 7-Mark KTU Questions:** Always draw the 2D matrix when explaining Herzberg. Examiners give bonus marks for visual representation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation: The Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham)

**Starting Premise:** Five core job characteristics influence three critical psychological states, which in turn determine work outcomes including satisfaction.

### **Step 1 — Five Core Job Characteristics**
1. **Skill Variety (SV)** — Degree to which a job requires multiple skills.
2. **Task Identity (TI)** — Degree to which a job involves completing a whole, identifiable piece of work.
3. **Task Significance (TS)** — Degree to which a job has a substantial impact on others.
4. **Autonomy (AU)** — Degree of freedom and discretion in scheduling work and deciding procedures.
5. **Feedback (FB)** — Degree to which performing the job provides direct, clear information about effectiveness.

### **Step 2 — Calculating the Motivating Potential Score (MPS)**

$$\text{MPS} = \frac{\text{SV} + \text{TI} + \text{TS}}{3} \times \text{AU} \times \text{FB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **MPS Range:** 1 to 336 (theoretical). Higher MPS = more motivating and satisfying job.

### **Step 3 — Three Critical Psychological States**
1. **Experienced Meaningfulness** (driven by SV, TI, TS)
2. **Experienced Responsibility** (driven by AU)
3. **Knowledge of Results** (driven by FB)

### **Step 4 — Work Outcomes**
When all three psychological states are present, employees experience:
- High **intrinsic motivation**
- High **job satisfaction**
- High **performance quality**
- Low **absenteeism & turnover**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Style:** When asked about JCM, always include the formula and explain that **autonomy and feedback act as multipliers** (they are weighted, not averaged).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Theory Applies When?

| Situation | Best Theory to Apply | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Employee complains about **low salary** | **Herzberg (Hygiene)** or **Equity Theory** | Pay is a hygiene factor; comparison drives equity feelings |
| Employee feels their **work is meaningless** | **Job Characteristics Model** | Task identity, significance, variety are missing |
| Employee is **bored** with repetitive tasks | **JCM / Job Enrichment** | Skill variety and autonomy need to be increased |
| Employee believes **promotions are unfair** | **Equity Theory (Adams)** | Outcome-to-input ratio imbalance |
| Employee wants **growth opportunities** | **Herzberg (Motivators)** | Recognition, growth are true motivators |
| Employee has **conflicting thoughts** about staying | **Cognitive Dissonance** | Inconsistency between behaviour and belief |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Best Practice:** Always link the *theory* to the *real-world situation* explicitly. This shows the examiner that you understand application, not just definition.
