# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination)** pattern, with internal choice, sub-parts, and proper mark distribution. Each question carries the **[KTU Past Year / Model Question]** tag for familiarity.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU Model – 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the term "attitude". Explain the cognitive component of attitude with a suitable workplace example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> An **attitude** is a learned predisposition to respond in a consistently favourable or unfavourable manner with respect to a given object, person, event, or idea (1 mark).

The **cognitive component** of attitude refers to the *beliefs, opinions, knowledge, and information* a person holds about the attitude object (1 mark).

**Workplace Example:** An engineer who believes "my organisation provides excellent career growth opportunities" (cognitive component) is exhibiting the cognitive dimension of their positive attitude toward the employer (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2 [KTU Model – 2024 Scheme]**
**Briefly explain any three factors affecting job satisfaction.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Three major factors affecting job satisfaction are:

1. **Pay and Compensation** — Fair and competitive salary directly increases satisfaction; underpayment causes dissatisfaction (1 mark).
2. **Quality of Supervision** — Supportive, considerate, and competent supervisors increase employee satisfaction (1 mark).
3. **Nature of Work Itself** — Jobs that offer variety, challenge, autonomy, and meaningful tasks lead to higher satisfaction (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts of (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model – 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the ABC model of attitude with a suitable diagram and workplace examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **ABC Model** of attitude, proposed by the standard OB literature, states that every attitude consists of three interlinked components:

**(i) Affective Component (Feelings) — 2 Marks**
This refers to the *emotional* segment of an attitude — how a person *feels* about the attitude object. It involves feelings of liking or disliking.

*Workplace Example:* An employee saying "I *love* coming to work every day" demonstrates a positive affective attitude.

**(ii) Behavioural Component (Intent to Act) — 2 Marks**
This refers to the *way* a person intends to behave toward the attitude object. It is the predisposition to act in a specific manner.

*Workplace Example:* An employee saying "I will *stay* with this organisation for the next decade" demonstrates the behavioural component.

**(iii) Cognitive Component (Beliefs/Knowledge) — 2 Marks**
This refers to the *beliefs, opinions, and knowledge* a person holds about the attitude object.

*Workplace Example:* An employee believing "this company is the market leader in its industry" demonstrates the cognitive component.

**(iv) Diagram — 1 Mark**

```mermaid
graph TD
    attObj[Attitude Object] --> affective[Affective Feelings]
    attObj --> cognitive[Cognitive Beliefs]
    attObj --> behavioural[Behavioural Intent to Act]
    affective --> overallAtt[Overall Attitude]
    cognitive --> overallAtt
    behavioural --> overallAtt
```

**(b) Discuss the major types of attitudes in organisational behaviour. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In organisational behaviour, the following types of attitudes are of critical importance:

**(1) Job Satisfaction — 1.5 Marks**
A positive emotional state resulting from the appraisal of one's job. It reflects how an employee feels about various facets of their work — pay, supervision, work itself, co-workers, and growth opportunities.

**(2) Job Involvement — 1.5 Marks**
The degree to which a person identifies psychologically with their job. Highly involved employees consider their job a *central part* of their self-concept and invest significant energy into it.

**(3) Organisational Commitment — 2 Marks**
The emotional attachment an employee has toward their organisation. Allen & Meyer (1991) identified three components:
- **Affective Commitment** — Emotional attachment ("I want to stay").
- **Continuance Commitment** — Cost-based attachment ("I need to stay").
- **Normative Commitment** — Moral obligation ("I ought to stay").

**(4) Perceived Organisational Support (POS) — 1 Mark**
The degree to which employees believe their organisation values their contribution and cares about their well-being. Higher POS leads to higher job satisfaction and commitment.

**(5) Employee Engagement — 1 Mark**
A higher-order construct representing the employee's *emotional and cognitive investment* in their work, going beyond mere satisfaction. Engaged employees actively contribute to organisational success.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Tip:** Always mention Allen & Meyer's three-component commitment model when discussing organisational commitment — it is a KTU favourite.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU Model – 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the determinants of job satisfaction in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Job satisfaction is determined by a complex interaction of multiple factors. These can be broadly classified as follows:

**(A) Personal Factors — 2.5 Marks**

1. **Personality** — Individuals with high *core self-evaluation* (positive self-concept) tend to report higher job satisfaction. Neurotic personalities tend to be less satisfied.
2. **Age** — Research shows a U-shaped relationship: satisfaction is high in early career, dips in mid-career, and rises again near retirement.
3. **Education Level** — Higher education often leads to *unmet expectations* and lower satisfaction.
4. **Emotional Intelligence (EI)** — Higher EI improves coping mechanisms and hence increases satisfaction.
5. **Gender** — Mixed evidence; modern research suggests only marginal differences.

**(B) Organisational / Job-Related Factors — 3 Marks**

1. **Pay and Compensation** — Fair, competitive, and transparent pay systems increase satisfaction.
2. **Nature of Work Itself** — Jobs offering variety, identity, significance, autonomy, and feedback lead to high satisfaction (per the Job Characteristics Model).
3. **Promotion Opportunities** — Career growth prospects strongly influence satisfaction.
4. **Supervision Quality** — Supportive, participative, and employee-oriented supervisors raise satisfaction.
5. **Co-worker Relations** — Positive peer relationships enhance satisfaction.
6. **Working Conditions** — Safe, comfortable, and ergonomically sound workplaces contribute to satisfaction.

**(C) Environmental / Extrinsic Factors — 1.5 Marks**

1. **Job Market Conditions** — When alternative jobs are scarce, employees may report *lower* satisfaction due to feeling "trapped."
2. **Economic Conditions** — Inflation, recession, and industry downturns affect satisfaction.
3. **Industry Trends** — Pay scales, work culture norms, and technological disruption influence satisfaction levels.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield:** In KTU 7-mark answers, present factors under clear sub-headings with at least one example per category.

---

**(b) Critically evaluate Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory of job satisfaction. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Herzberg's **Two-Factor Theory** (1959), also called the **Motivation-Hygiene Theory**, was developed by Frederick Herzberg through a study of 200 engineers and accountants in Pittsburgh.

**(i) Core Premise — 1 Mark**
Herzberg proposed that job satisfaction and dissatisfaction are **NOT opposites of the same continuum** but are caused by **two distinct, independent sets of factors.**

**(ii) Motivator Factors (Intrinsic) — 1.5 Marks**
These lead to **satisfaction** when present. They include:
- Achievement
- Recognition
- The work itself
- Responsibility
- Advancement
- Growth

**(iii) Hygiene Factors (Extrinsic) — 1.5 Marks**
These prevent **dissatisfaction** when adequate. Their absence causes dissatisfaction. They include:
- Pay and compensation
- Company policies and administration
- Supervision quality
- Working conditions
- Interpersonal relations
- Job security

**(iv) 2D Matrix Representation — 1 Mark**

| | **High Hygiene** | **Low Hygiene** |
|---|---|---|
| **High Motivators** | Ideal (Satisfied + No Dissatisfaction) | Motivated but Dissatisfied |
| **Low Motivators** | Comfortable but Unmotivated | Worst State |

**(v) Critical Evaluation — 2 Marks**

**Strengths:**
1. First theory to recognise that satisfaction and dissatisfaction are *separate* dimensions.
2. Has had a major influence on **job enrichment** programmes in industry.
3. Simple to understand and apply by line managers.

**Limitations:**
1. The methodology (critical incident technique) has been criticised for being **subjective**.
2. The sample (engineers and accountants) is not representative of all occupations.
3. The theory **ignores individual differences** — different people value different factors.
4. The dichotomy between motivator and hygiene factors is **not always clear-cut** (e.g., pay can be a motivator in some contexts).
5. The theory may be **culturally biased** — the original study was conducted in the United States.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 7-Mark Best Practice:** Always provide *both* strengths and limitations. An answer with only praise is marked down for being uncritical.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 OB Exams:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Attitude and Behaviour** — Attitude is a *predisposition*, not actual behaviour. Many students wrongly write that attitude = behaviour.
> 2. **Forgetting Allen & Meyer** — When asked about organisational commitment, mentioning only "loyalty" without the three-component model (Affective, Continuance, Normative) loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Job Satisfaction with Job Involvement** — Job satisfaction is about *feelings* toward the job; job involvement is about *psychological identification* with the job.
> 4. **Ignoring Limitations** — When explaining theories (Herzberg, Equity, etc.), always give **at least 2 limitations**. KTU examiners reward critical thinking.
> 5. **Not Drawing Diagrams** — In 7-mark and 14-mark questions, a **diagram (mermaid-style, flowchart, or simple sketch)** earns easy bonus marks.
> 6. **Misquoting the Theory** — Herzberg's theory says *satisfaction and dissatisfaction are independent*, not opposites. Students often write "hygiene factors cause satisfaction and motivators cause dissatisfaction" — this is the OPPOSITE of what Herzberg said.
> 7. **Skipping Examples** — Every theoretical point should be supported by a *workplace example*. Theory without application is marked down.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Attitude** = Learned + Predisposition + Evaluative (favourable/unfavourable).
- ✅ **ABC Model** = Affective (feelings) + Behavioural (intent to act) + Cognitive (beliefs).
- ✅ **Job Satisfaction** = Positive emotional state from job appraisal (Locke).
- ✅ **Job Satisfaction Formula:** $S = P - D$ (Perceived minus Desired outcomes).
- ✅ **Job Involvement** = Psychological identification with the job.
- ✅ **Organisational Commitment (Allen & Meyer)** = Affective + Continuance + Normative.
- ✅ **Attitude Formation** = Direct experience + Social learning + Conditioning + Cognitive processing.
- ✅ **Cognitive Dissonance (Festinger)** = Discomfort from inconsistent cognitions, resolved via behaviour change, attitude change, or new cognition.
- ✅ **Job Satisfaction Determinants** = Personal + Organisational + Environmental factors.
- ✅ **Outcomes of Job Satisfaction** = Performance, OCB, low absenteeism, low turnover, high life satisfaction.
- ✅ **Performance ↔ Satisfaction** = Performance leads to satisfaction more strongly (modern view).
- ✅ **Equity Theory (Adams)** = Compare outcome-to-input ratio with referent others.
- ✅ **Herzberg Two-Factor** = Motivators (intrinsic) cause satisfaction; Hygiene (extrinsic) prevents dissatisfaction.
- ✅ **JCM (Hackman & Oldham)** = 5 characteristics (SV, TI, TS, AU, FB) → 3 psychological states → work outcomes.
- ✅ **MPS Formula:** $\text{MPS} = \frac{\text{SV} + \text{TI} + \text{TS}}{3} \times \text{AU} \times \text{FB}$.
- ✅ **Always give limitations** when explaining theories.
- ✅ **Always use diagrams** in 7+ mark answers.
- ✅ **Always include workplace examples** for every theoretical point.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Individual Behaviour: Attitudes and Job Satisfaction**
> *Prepared strictly per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern and Board Examiner standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Attitude
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** UEHUT803 - ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOUR AND BUSINESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Components of Attitude # 🧠 Components of Attitude — Foundational Concepts > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a direct continuation of the "Attitude" concept introduced in Module 2 of UEHUT803. It is frequently tested in Part A (3-mark) questions and forms the theoretical backbone for Part B (14-mark) questions on Attitude, Job Satisfaction, and Organizational Commitment. ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Organizational Behaviour (OB)**, attitude is defined as a **psychological tendency** expressed by evaluating a particular entity (person, place, thing, or idea) with some degree of **favour or disfavour**. Every attitude, by definition, is a composite structure built from three interlocking internal components. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Tri-Component Model of Attitude (Standard KTU Definition):** > Attitude is a learned, relatively enduring predisposition to respond consistently in a favourable or unfavourable manner toward a specific object, person, idea, or situation. It consists of three components: > 1. **Cognitive Component** (The "Thinking" Part) > 2. **Affective Component** (The "Feeling" Part) > 3. **Behavioural (Conative) Component** (The "Acting" Part) This model is popularly attributed to the classic work on attitude structure in social psychology and is adopted in KTU 2024 OB textbooks (often called the **ABC Model of Attitude**). ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Coffee Cup" Analogy Imagine you walk into a new café and form an attitude about it. Three things happen inside your head **simultaneously**: - ☕ **You THINK something** about the café — "I've read online that they use freshly roasted Arabica beans." (This is the **Cognitive** part — your belief or knowledge.) - ☕ **You FEEL something** — "That makes me happy and excited to try it." (This is the **Affective** part — your emotion.) - ☕ **You DO something** — "I will walk in, sit down, and order a cappuccino." (This is the **Behavioural** part — your intention to act.) > [!TIP] > **Student Memory Trick — "CAB" or "ABC":** > **C**ognition → **A**ffect → **B**ehaviour. > Think = Cognition, Feel = Affect, Act = Behaviour. > All three together = one complete attitude. ## 1.3 Why Components Matter in an Organizational Setting In KTU UEHUT803, you are expected to link these components to workplace outcomes: | Workplace Object | Cognitive (Belief) | Affective (Feeling) | Behavioural (Action) | |---|---|---|---| | New HR Policy | "This policy reduces workload." | "I feel relieved and less stressed." | "I will comply and support its rollout." | | Toxic Boss | "My manager plays favourites." | "I feel angry and demotivated." | "I will start looking for a new job." | | Flexible Work Hours | "Output is judged, not hours." | "I feel trusted and valued." | "I will work harder and stay later when needed." | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** KTU examiners note that students often *conflate* attitude with behaviour. Remember — **attitude is internal (latent)**; **behaviour is external (observable)**. Attitude *predicts* behaviour but is not behaviour itself. # 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Three Components ## 2.1 The Cognitive Component (The "Knowing" Element) ### 🔹 Definition The **cognitive component** of attitude refers to the **beliefs, thoughts, ideas, knowledge, and information** a person holds about an attitude object. It is the **rational, logical appraisal** based on perceptions, facts, and prior learning. ### 🔹 Characteristics - It is **knowledge-based** and **information-driven**. - It is the **foundation** upon which feelings and actions are built. - It is formed through **learning, observation, and experience**. - It is the most **changeable** component because new information can directly alter beliefs. ### 🔹 KTU-Relevant Examples - "A postgraduate degree increases salary potential." (Belief about education) - "CSR activities improve brand image." (Belief about corporate social responsibility) - "Remote work reduces commute stress." (Belief about flexible work) > [!NOTE] > **OB Insight:** When HR professionals conduct **training and development** programs, they are primarily trying to shift the **cognitive component** — by providing new information, they hope to update employee beliefs, which then trigger new feelings and behaviours. --- ## 2.2 The Affective Component (The "Feeling" Element) ### 🔹 Definition The **affective component** refers to the **emotions, feelings, moods, and sentiments** a person associates with the attitude object. It represents the **evaluative emotional tone** — whether the person feels positively or negatively toward the object. ### 🔹 Characteristics - It is **emotionally driven** rather than logically driven. - It is often the **strongest and most persistent** of the three components. - It is **resistant to change** because emotions are deeply ingrained. - It is responsible for the **"gut feeling"** aspect of attitude. ### 🔹 KTU-Relevant Examples - "I love working at Infosys — it feels like family." (Positive affect toward employer) - "I dread Monday morning meetings." (Negative affect toward meetings) - "I feel proud wearing my company ID." (Positive affect toward organization) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Affective-Cognitive Consistency Principle (Cognitive Dissonance):** > When beliefs and feelings are misaligned, employees experience **Cognitive Dissonance** (Festinger, 1957). KTU frequently tests this concept in Module 2 questions on attitude change and workplace motivation. --- ## 2.3 The Behavioural (Conative) Component (The "Acting" Element) ### 🔹 Definition The **behavioural (or conative) component** refers to the **intentions, tendencies, and observable actions** a person takes or plans to take toward the attitude object. It is the **predisposition to act** in a specific way. ### 🔹 Characteristics - It is the **only directly observable** component. - It manifests as **intentions, verbal statements of action, or actual conduct**. - It is the **most measurable** component in OB research. - It is influenced by the cognitive and affective components. ### 🔹 KTU-Relevant Examples - "I will recommend my company as a great place to work on Glassdoor." (Behavioural intention) - "I will resign within 6 months." (Behavioural intent) - "I actively participate in team meetings." (Observable behaviour) > [!TIP] > **The Intention–Behaviour Gap:** > KTU examiners love this nuance. **Behavioural intention ≠ Actual behaviour.** Many employees *intend* to stay but leave; many *intend* to perform but don't. Organizational Behaviour studies this gap using models like the **Theory of Planned Behaviour (Ajzen)**. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Summary Table — The ABC Model | Attribute | Cognitive | Affective | Behavioural (Conative) | |---|---|---|---| | **Core Question** | "What do I believe?" | "How do I feel?" | "What will I do?" | | **Nature** | Rational, logical | Emotional, evaluative | Intentional, action-oriented | | **Source** | Learning & information | Emotions & moods | Motivations & intentions | | **Visibility** | Internal (latent) | Internal (latent) | External (observable) | | **Persistence** | Moderate | Very High | Moderate to Low | | **Ease of Change** | High (via new info) | Low (resistant) | Variable | | **OB Example** | Beliefs about job security | Pride in company | Decision to stay or leave | | **HR Lever** | Training, communication | Recognition, culture | Incentives, role design | | **KTU Exam Weight** | 30% | 35% | 35% | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility In the KTU 2024 scheme, this model is not just theoretical — it is applied across: - **Human Resource Management (HRM):** Designing attitude surveys (e.g., the *Job Descriptive Index*, *Minnesota Satisfaction Questionnaire*). - **Marketing:** Predicting customer purchase behaviour through the **Tri-Component Model** (Belief → Preference → Purchase). - **Organizational Development:** Diagnosing why employees resist change (cognitive bias, emotional attachment, or behavioural habit). - **Industrial Psychology:** Measuring **Job Satisfaction** as an attitude (a job satisfaction survey measures all three components). > [!NOTE] > **Cross-Module Link:** This ABC Model feeds directly into the Module 2 topics of *Attitude Formation*, *Attitude Change*, *Job Satisfaction*, and *Organizational Commitment* — all of which are common Part B questions in KTU ESE. # 🔬 Analytical Matrix — Operational Logic of the Components ## 3.1 How the Three Components Interact (The Causal Chain) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Standard KTU Causal Sequence:** > **Cognition → Affect → Behaviour** > Beliefs generate feelings, and feelings drive actions. This is a **directional, sequential relationship**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to demonstrate this through workplace case studies. ### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction **Step 1 — Cognitive Input (Belief Formation)** The employee receives information from formal (training, policies) and informal (gossip, observation) sources. The brain encodes this as a *belief statement*. $$B = f(\text{Information, Past Experience, Social Influence})$$ Where: - $B$ = Belief (cognitive content) - $f$ = A function combining informational inputs **Step 2 — Cognitive-Affective Translation** The belief is evaluated against personal values, needs, and prior emotions, generating an affective response. $$A = g(B, V, N)$$ Where: - $A$ = Affect (emotional evaluation) - $V$ = Personal values - $N$ = Needs (Maslow, Alderfer frameworks) - $g$ = A cognitive-emotional mapping function **Step 3 — Affective-Behavioural Translation** The emotional state combines with situational constraints to produce an intention or action. $$I = h(A, C)$$ Where: - $I$ = Intention (behavioural tendency) - $C$ = Contextual/situational constraints (time, resources, norms) - $h$ = A behavioural activation function **Step 4 — Intention to Behaviour Translation (Ajzen's TPB Extension)** The intention may or may not translate into actual behaviour, depending on **Perceived Behavioural Control (PBC)**. $$B_{\text{actual}} = p \cdot I$$ Where: - $p$ = Perceived behavioural control factor, $0 \leq p \leq 1$ - $B_{\text{actual}}$ = The observable behaviour > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. You may be asked to map your answer to **Course Outcomes (COs)** such as: > - **CO2:** "Demonstrate the ability to apply behavioural science concepts in organizational settings." > - **CO3:** "Analyze human behaviour at individual, group, and organizational levels." > Always anchor your answers to these COs when possible. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Workplace Case Analysis ### Case Scenario: *A Mid-Level Manager, Priya, Reacts to a Newly Announced "Return-to-Office" Policy* | Component | Internal Process | Priya's Specific Response | OB Concept Tested | |---|---|---|---| | **Cognitive (Belief)** | She recalls news articles on productivity loss. | "I believe hybrid work is more efficient for knowledge workers like me." | Belief formation, Information processing | | **Affective (Feeling)** | Her belief clashes with her need for work-life balance. | "I feel anxious, frustrated, and betrayed by leadership." | Emotional labour, Cognitive dissonance | | **Behavioural (Action)** | She considers her options and chooses an action. | "I will start updating my LinkedIn and attend a competitor's hiring drive." | Turnover intention, Job search behaviour | ### Sequential Causal Flow (Inferred from Case) $$\text{Belief (Hybrid is better)} \longrightarrow \text{Feeling (Anxious, Betrayed)} \longrightarrow \text{Intention (Search for new job)} \longrightarrow \text{Behaviour (Update LinkedIn, Attend Interview)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Alert:** Some KTU students mistakenly treat the three components as **independent** rather than **interdependent**. Always emphasize the **interaction and influence** each component has on the others. --- ## 3.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of Attitude Structure Attitude can be visualized as a **three-layer pyramid**, with the cognitive base supporting the affective middle, which in turn drives the behavioural apex. - **Layer 1 (Base — Cognitive):** Knowledge and beliefs. The most stable foundation because it is built on information. - **Layer 2 (Middle — Affective):** Emotions and feelings. The most influential layer because emotions color the entire attitude. - **Layer 3 (Apex — Behavioural):** Action and intention. The most visible and measurable layer. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Exams — "K**now → **E**motion → **A**ction = **KEA**": > *Know what you believe, Feel what you value, Act on what you intend.* --- ## 3.4 The Moderating Factors Between Components The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes that the C → A → B chain is **not automatic**. It is moderated by: | Moderator | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Personal Values** | Deep-seated principles (Schwartz Values) | "Fairness" intensifies negative feelings toward favouritism. | | **Cultural Norms** | Societal/Organizational culture | Collectivist cultures may suppress negative behavioural reactions. | | **Self-Interest** | Personal benefit calculation | A profitable policy may be liked even if perceived as unfair. | | **Reference Groups** | Peer influence | Friends' opinions shift personal beliefs. | | **Situation** | Immediate context | Stress at home may amplify negative affective responses. | | **Personality** | Dispositional traits | High *Openness* makes cognitive reassessment easier. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Application Question Trigger:** > If an exam question says, *"Why do two employees with the same beliefs about a policy react differently?"* — your answer must invoke these **moderators**. This is a high-mark-scoring insight. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Tri-Component Model (ABC Model) — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A["COGNITIVE COMPONENT
Beliefs and Knowledge
What I think and know"] --> B["AFFECTIVE COMPONENT
Feelings and Emotions
What I feel about it"] B --> C["BEHAVIOURAL COMPONENT
Intentions and Actions
What I will do about it"] A -.Influences.-> C C -.Reinforces or Revises.-> A A -.Colors.-> B B -.Motivates.-> C C -.Confirms.-> A style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#E67E22,stroke:#784212,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#27AE60,stroke:#196F3D,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Attitude Formation to Behavioural Outcome — Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Stage 1: Information
Acquisition"] --> P2["Stage 2: Belief
Formation"] P2 --> P3["Stage 3: Emotional
Evaluation"] P3 --> P4["Stage 4: Behavioural
Intention"] P4 --> P5{"Stage 5: Perceived
Behavioural Control?"} P5 -- High Control --> P6["Stage 6A: Actual
Behaviour"] P5 -- Low Control --> P7["Stage 6B: Intention
Stalled or Suppressed"] P6 --> P8["Stage 7: Outcome
and Feedback"] P7 --> P8 P8 --> P1 style P1 fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#FFFFFF style P2 fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#FFFFFF style P3 fill:#E67E22,stroke:#784212,color:#FFFFFF style P4 fill:#E67E22,stroke:#784212,color:#FFFFFF style P5 fill:#F39C12,stroke:#7D6608,color:#FFFFFF style P6 fill:#27AE60,stroke:#196F3D,color:#FFFFFF style P7 fill:#C0392B,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF style P8 fill:#8E44AD,stroke:#4A235A,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.3 Moderators of the Cognitive-Affective-Behavioural Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD Core["Tri-Component
Attitude Chain
Cognition - Affect - Behaviour"] Core --> M1["Personal Values"] Core --> M2["Cultural Norms"] Core --> M3["Self Interest"] Core --> M4["Reference Groups"] Core --> M5["Situational Context"] Core --> M6["Personality Traits"] M1 --> Out["Workplace Outcomes:
Performance, Turnover,
Engagement, OCB"] M2 --> Out M3 --> Out M4 --> Out M5 --> Out M6 --> Out style Core fill:#34495E,stroke:#1B2631,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px style M1 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#1B4F72 style M2 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#1B4F72 style M3 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#1B4F72 style M4 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#1B4F72 style M5 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#1B4F72 style M6 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#1B4F72 style Out fill:#27AE60,stroke:#196F3D,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 ABC Model Mapped to OB Concepts ```mermaid mindmap root((ABC Model
of Attitude)) Cognitive Beliefs Knowledge Stereotypes Perceptions Affective Feelings Emotions Mood Sentiment Behavioural Intentions Verbal Stances Actual Actions OCB Applications Job Satisfaction Org Commitment Consumer Behaviour Resistance to Change ``` # ✍️ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and require concise 3-4 sentence answers. Each is mapped to a specific Bloom's cognitive level (typically *Remember* or *Understand*). --- ### **Question 1: Define the three components of attitude with suitable examples.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Attitude is a learned predisposition to respond favourably or unfavourably toward an attitude object. It consists of three components: 1. **Cognitive Component:** Refers to the beliefs, thoughts, and knowledge a person holds about an object. *Example:* An employee believes that "performance-based pay increases motivation." 2. **Affective Component:** Refers to the emotions and feelings associated with the object. *Example:* The same employee "feels excited and motivated" by the new pay structure. 3. **Behavioural Component:** Refers to the intentions and observable actions toward the object. *Example:* The employee "decides to work longer hours and take on extra projects" to maximize the incentive. > [!TIP] > **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark per component. Use a workplace example for each to score full marks. --- ### **Question 2: Differentiate between the cognitive and affective components of attitude.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | Cognitive Component | Affective Component | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Beliefs and knowledge about an object | Feelings and emotions about an object | | **Nature** | Rational, logical, fact-based | Emotional, evaluative, subjective | | **Source** | Learning, information, observation | Personal values, experiences, mood | | **Example** | "Diversity hiring improves innovation." | "I feel proud to work in an inclusive team." | Both components jointly determine behaviour, but the affective component is usually more persistent and resistant to change. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) Explain the Tri-Component Model of Attitude in detail with workplace examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The Tri-Component Model of Attitude, also known as the **ABC Model**, was developed to explain the multi-dimensional nature of attitudes. It posits that every attitude has three interrelated components: **1. Cognitive Component (7 Marks total breakdown):** - Represents the **beliefs, ideas, knowledge, and perceptions** a person holds about an object. - It is the *thinking* part of attitude — the rational appraisal based on facts and prior information. - **Workplace Example:** A sales manager believes that "the new CRM software reduces customer response time by 30%." This belief is based on training data and vendor demonstrations. - The cognitive component is **changeable** through new information, training, and communication. **2. Affective Component:** - Represents the **emotions, feelings, and sentiments** associated with the object. - It is the *feeling* part of attitude — the evaluative emotional tone. - **Workplace Example:** The same sales manager *feels enthusiastic and confident* about the new CRM because it aligns with her professional goals. - The affective component is the **most resistant to change** because emotions are deeply embedded. **3. Behavioural (Conative) Component:** - Represents the **intentions, tendencies, and observable actions** toward the object. - It is the *acting* part of attitude — the predisposition to behave in a particular way. - **Workplace Example:** The sales manager *voluntarily enrolls in the CRM training program* and encourages her team to do the same. - The behavioural component is the **only directly observable** and **measurable** component. **Conclusion:** The three components are **interdependent and mutually reinforcing**. Beliefs trigger emotions, emotions drive actions, and the outcomes of actions provide feedback that may revise beliefs and feelings. --- **(b) "Behavioural intention is not the same as actual behaviour." Critically analyse this statement using the Tri-Component Model. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** This statement is a key insight from the **Theory of Planned Behaviour (Ajzen, 1991)** and is highly relevant to organizational settings. **Step 1 — The Intention-Behaviour Gap:** The behavioural component of attitude includes both *intentions* (what one *plans* to do) and *actual behaviour* (what one *does*). However, intentions do not always translate into action due to intervening variables. **Step 2 — The Role of Perceived Behavioural Control:** According to Ajzen, the translation of intention into behaviour depends on **Perceived Behavioural Control (PBC)** — the person's belief about how easy or difficult it will be to perform the behaviour. **Step 3 — Mathematical Representation (Optional but KTU-Appreciated):** $$B_{\text{actual}} = p \cdot I$$ Where: - $B_{\text{actual}}$ = Observed behaviour - $I$ = Behavioural intention - $p$ = Perceived Behavioural Control factor, $0 \leq p \leq 1$ If $p = 1$, behaviour perfectly matches intention. If $p < 1$, behaviour is **suppressed or modified**. **Step 4 — Workplace Example:** An employee may *intend* to stay with the organization (positive behavioural intention) but *actually resign* (different behaviour) because: - Better external job offers reduce PBC to stay. - Family relocation creates an external constraint. - Poor health reduces energy to commit. **Step 5 — Implications for HR:** HR managers must measure **actual behaviour** (e.g., absenteeism, turnover, performance) and not rely solely on **attitude surveys** that capture intentions. **Conclusion:** The Tri-Component Model teaches us that the *behavioural* component is a **spectrum from intention to action**, and OB professionals must distinguish between the two for accurate diagnosis and intervention. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) Discuss the role of the affective component in shaping employee attitudes and workplace behaviour. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **affective component** is the emotional heart of attitude. It refers to the *feelings, moods, and sentiments* an employee associates with work, the organization, colleagues, and policies. **Sub-topic 1: Sources of Affect at Work** - **Job Satisfaction** as an affective evaluation of one's job. - **Organizational Commitment** as an affective bond with the organization (Affective Commitment, per Meyer & Allen's Three-Component Model). - **Emotional Labour** — the management of feelings to meet job role demands (Hochschild). **Sub-topic 2: Influence on Workplace Behaviour** - Positive affect → Higher engagement, Organizational Citizenship Behaviour (OCB), lower turnover. - Negative affect → Withdrawal, absenteeism, sabotage, deviant workplace behaviour. - Affect is a **stronger predictor of behaviour** than cognition in emotionally charged decisions (e.g., harassment complaints, whistleblowing). **Sub-topic 3: Management of Affect in OB** - **Job enrichment** to generate positive feelings. - **Recognition and rewards** to boost emotional engagement. - **Counselling and Employee Assistance Programs (EAPs)** to manage negative emotions. **Conclusion:** The affective component is the **most powerful and persistent** driver of attitude, and OB interventions aimed at emotional well-being (e.g., positive organizational culture) yield the highest behavioural returns. --- **(b) Explain how the three components of attitude interact to produce workplace outcomes such as job satisfaction and turnover. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Interaction Logic:** The three components interact in a **causal chain**: $$\text{Cognition (Belief)} \longrightarrow \text{Affect (Feeling)} \longrightarrow \text{Behaviour (Action)}$$ **Step 2 — Application to Job Satisfaction:** - *Cognition:* Employee believes "this organization values my contributions." - *Affect:* Employee "feels satisfied and proud." - *Behaviour:* Employee "performs at a high level, stays long-term, and recommends the organization." **Step 3 — Application to Turnover:** - *Cognition:* Employee believes "my skills are not being utilized here." - *Affect:* Employee "feels frustrated and under-valued." - *Behaviour:* Employee "begins job search, attends interviews, and eventually resigns." **Step 4 — The Moderating Role:** Personal values, organizational culture, and situational factors can **amplify or dampen** each component's effect. For instance, a strong belief in fairness may amplify negative feelings if the employee perceives inequity. **Step 5 — OB Implications:** - **Cognitive interventions:** Job previews, training, transparent communication. - **Affective interventions:** Wellness programs, supportive supervision. - **Behavioural interventions:** Performance-based rewards, career development tracks. **Conclusion:** Job satisfaction and turnover are not random — they are the **predictable outcomes of a well-functioning or dysfunctional ABC chain**. OB interventions that target all three components simultaneously produce the most sustainable results. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Treating components as independent** — Always show the **interaction** between C, A, and B. This is where 2-3 marks are lost. > 2. **Confusing attitude with behaviour** — Attitude is **internal**; behaviour is **external**. Many students blur this distinction and lose a mark. > 3. **Skipping workplace examples** — The KTU 2024 scheme uses **OBE-based evaluation**. Generic answers without workplace illustrations score lower. > 4. **Ignoring Course Outcome mapping** — Where possible, signal which CO your answer addresses (e.g., "This analysis demonstrates CO3: analyzing human behaviour at the individual level"). > 5. **Forgetting the intention-behaviour gap** — When discussing the behavioural component, always note that **intention ≠ actual action**. > 6. **Mere definitions without analysis** — The 14-mark question requires **explanation + analysis + application + conclusion**. Skipping any layer costs marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **🟢 Final 5-Minute Revision Block — Memorize Before the Exam:** - ✅ **Definition:** Attitude is a *learned, relatively enduring predisposition* to evaluate an object favourably or unfavourably. - ✅ **Three Components:** **Cognitive (Belief)** + **Affective (Feeling)** + **Behavioural (Intention/Action)** = The **ABC Model** or **Tri-Component Model**. - ✅ **Cognitive:** Beliefs, knowledge, thoughts, perceptions. *Source: Learning. Easily changed.* - ✅ **Affective:** Emotions, feelings, sentiments, moods. *Most persistent. Hardest to change.* - ✅ **Behavioural:** Intentions and observable actions. *Only directly measurable component.* - ✅ **Causal Chain:** Cognition → Affect → Behaviour (Belief → Feeling → Action). - ✅ **Intention ≠ Behaviour:** Mediated by *Perceived Behavioural Control* (Ajzen's TPB). - ✅ **Workplace Applications:** Job Satisfaction, Organizational Commitment, OCB, Turnover, Customer Behaviour. - ✅ **Moderators:** Personal values, culture, self-interest, reference groups, situation, personality. - ✅ **Hierarchy:** The three components form a *pyramid* — Cognitive (base) → Affective (middle) → Behavioural (apex). - ✅ **OB Levers:** Training changes **Cognition**; Recognition changes **Affect**; Incentives change **Behaviour**. - ✅ **Mnemonic — "KEA":** **K**now (Cognition) → **E**motion (Affect) → **A**ction (Behaviour). - ✅ **KTU Course Outcomes Linked:** CO2 (Apply OB concepts), CO3 (Analyze human behaviour). - ✅ **Common Exam Triggers:** "Tri-component model," "ABC of attitude," "Cognitive vs Affective vs Behavioural," "Why attitude ≠ behaviour." - ✅ **Theorists to Reference:** Ajzen (Theory of Planned Behaviour), Festinger (Cognitive Dissonance), Meyer & Allen (Affective Commitment), Hochschild (Emotional Labour). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Wisdom:** When answering a KTU 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer in **four clear layers**: > 1. **Definition** (2 marks) > 2. **Explanation of each component with workplace example** (6 marks) > 3. **Analysis of how components interact / application to a real scenario** (4 marks) > 4. **Conclusion linking to OB and CO mapping** (2 marks) > > This structure aligns with the KTU 2024 OBE-based valuation key and maximizes your score.

topics.title: Personality and Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT803 - ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOUR AND BUSINESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Personality and Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Personality — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personality** is the dynamic and organized set of characteristics possessed by a person that uniquely influences their cognitions, motivations, emotions, and behavior in various situations.

In the KTU 2024 organizational behaviour framework, personality is treated as the **"psychological architecture"** of an individual — the relatively stable pattern of thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that emerges from the interaction of internal dispositions and external environmental stimuli. It is what makes a person *predictable* in behaviour across time, yet *flexible* across different social contexts.

The word *personality* is derived from the **Latin word "Persona"**, which refers to the **mask** worn by actors in Greek and Roman theatre to project a specific role or character. Just as the mask concealed the actor's true face while revealing a chosen identity, modern psychology views personality as the outward expression of inner psychological patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Proposition (Allport, 1961):** Personality is *"the dynamic organization within the individual of those psychophysical systems that determine his characteristic behavior and thought."*

---

### 1.2 Values — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Values** are enduring beliefs that specify a mode of conduct (instrumental value) or an end-state of existence (terminal value) that is personally or socially preferable to an opposite or converse mode of conduct or end-state of existence. *(Rokeach, 1973)*

Values are the **guiding principles** that act as the standard criteria people use to evaluate events, people, and actions as good, bad, right, or wrong. They form the deepest layer of an individual's cognitive structure — deeper than attitudes and opinions, and resistant to short-term change.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy 1 — The "Software + Operating System" Metaphor:**
Think of a person as a **computer**:
- **Personality** is the **software** (e.g., Word, Excel, Photoshop) — it determines *how* you process and react to inputs.
- **Values** are the **Operating System** (e.g., Windows, Linux, macOS) — it determines *why* you choose one software over another, what your default rules of operation are, and what is permitted or forbidden at the root level.
- The **situation** is the **hardware environment** — it can constrain or enhance what the software can do, but cannot rewrite the OS.

**Analogy 2 — The "House" Metaphor:**
- **Personality** = the *architecture* of a house (number of floors, layout, style).
- **Values** = the *foundation* on which the house is built.
- **Behaviour** = the *furniture and décor* inside — these change frequently, but the architecture and foundation remain.

**Analogy 3 — Real-World Workplace Example:**
Consider two managers handling a layoff situation:
- Manager A (High Agreeableness personality + Terminal value of "Equality") may struggle emotionally with the decision.
- Manager B (High Conscientiousness personality + Terminal value of "Organizational Success") may execute the decision efficiently.
**Same situation, different outcomes** — because of the interaction of personality traits and underlying value systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, *Personality* answers the question **"How will the person behave?"**, while *Values* answer the question **"Why does the person want to behave that way?"**. Examiners love this distinction.

---

### 1.4 Key Distinctions at a Glance

| Aspect | Personality | Values |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Combination of traits & temperament | Enduring beliefs about goals & conduct |
| **Depth** | Surface-to-Middle level | Deepest cognitive layer |
| **Stability** | Stable in adulthood, but adaptable | Highly stable across lifespan |
| **Quantity** | Composed of many traits (e.g., 5 in Big Five) | Limited core set (e.g., ~18 in Rokeach) |
| **Measurement** | Personality tests (MBTI, Big Five) | Value surveys (Rokeach Value Survey, Schwartz) |
| **Answers** | *How* one acts | *Why* one acts |
| **KTU Cognitive Level** | Understand / Apply | Remember / Understand |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Determinants of Personality

Personality is shaped by a complex interaction of three major forces. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to articulate these determinants and provide at least one real-world example for each.

#### A. Heredity (Nature)
- Refers to the **biological and genetic endowment** received from parents.
- Influences physical appearance, energy levels, intelligence quotient (IQ), and certain temperamental traits (e.g., introversion/extroversion tendencies).
- **Key argument:** Twins raised apart show remarkable personality similarity, suggesting genetic influence.

#### B. Environment (Nurture)
The cultural, social, familial, and educational surroundings in which a person is raised.
- **Culture:** Shapes norms, worldview, and acceptable behaviour.
- **Family:** The primary agent of socialization; parenting style (authoritative, authoritarian, permissive) shapes self-esteem and discipline.
- **Social Class:** Influences opportunities, language patterns, and aspirations.
- **Education & Religion:** Instil discipline, moral frameworks, and intellectual orientation.

#### C. Situation
- The **immediate context** can temporarily override dispositional tendencies.
- A normally extraverted person may become reserved in a high-stakes interview.
- The **Person-Situation Interaction** is now widely accepted — behaviour is a function of *both* the person and the situation:
  $$ B = f(P, S, P \times S) $$
  where $B$ = Behaviour, $P$ = Person (personality), $S$ = Situation, and $P \times S$ = their interaction effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** The "Person–Situation Debate" was championed by **Walter Mischel (1968)**, who challenged the consistency of personality. Modern OB accepts an **interactionist perspective** — personality *and* situation jointly determine behaviour.

---

### 2.2 The Big Five Personality Model (OCEAN Model)

The **Five-Factor Model (FFM)** is the most scientifically validated personality framework. It is widely tested in KTU examinations because of its clarity and empirical strength.

| # | Trait | High Scorer Profile | Low Scorer Profile | Workplace Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **O** | **Openness to Experience** | Curious, creative, imaginative, original | Conventional, practical, prefers routine | Suited for R&D, design, strategy roles |
| **C** | **Conscientiousness** | Organized, reliable, disciplined, goal-oriented | Impulsive, careless, disorganized | Best predictor of job performance across occupations |
| **E** | **Extraversion** | Sociable, talkative, assertive, energetic | Reserved, quiet, introspective | Sales, leadership, networking roles |
| **A** | **Agreeableness** | Trusting, cooperative, soft-hearted, helpful | Skeptical, competitive, tough-minded | Teamwork, customer service, HR roles |
| **N** | **Neuroticism** | Anxious, insecure, emotional, moody | Calm, self-confident, resilient | Linked to burnout risk; low scorers cope better with stress |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use the mnemonic OCEAN** — *Open, Conscientious, Extraverted, Agreeable, Neurotic* — to recall the five factors in KTU exams.

**Empirical Highlights:**
- **Conscientiousness** is the single **best predictor of job performance** across all occupational groups (Barrick & Mount, 1991).
- **Extraversion** strongly predicts success in **sales and managerial** roles.
- **Openness** predicts **creative and innovative** performance.
- High **Neuroticism** correlates with **turnover intentions and burnout**.

---

### 2.3 The Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI) — Overview

The MBTI, based on **Carl Jung's theory of psychological types**, classifies people into 16 personality types using four dichotomies:

| Dichotomy | Pole A | Pole B |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Extraversion (E) | Introversion (I) |
| **Information Gathering** | Sensing (S) | Intuition (N) |
| **Decision Making** | Thinking (T) | Feeling (F) |
| **Lifestyle Orientation** | Judging (J) | Perceiving (P) |

**Example type:** **ESTJ** = Extraverted, Sensing, Thinking, Judging (often called "The Supervisor" — organized, decisive, traditional leaders).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Critical Note:** MBTI is **not scientifically supported** as rigorously as the Big Five. It has poor test-retest reliability (~50% get different results within 5 years). Examiners may ask students to **critically evaluate** MBTI versus Big Five.

---

### 2.4 Rokeach Value Survey (Rokeach, 1973)

Milton Rokeach classified values into two distinct categories:

#### Terminal Values (End-States)
Desirable **goals** or end-states of existence that a person strives to achieve.
*Examples:* A World at Peace, Family Security, Freedom, Equality, True Friendship, Salvation, Self-Respect, Happiness, Wisdom, Prosperity, National Security, Mature Love, Pleasure, An Exciting Life, A Sense of Accomplishment, Social Recognition, Inner Harmony, Beauty, Comfortable Life.

#### Instrumental Values (Means)
Preferable **modes of conduct** or means by which terminal values are achieved.
*Examples:* Ambitious, Broad-Minded, Capable, Cheerful, Clean, Courageous, Forgiving, Helpful, Honest, Imaginative, Independent, Intellectual, Logical, Loving, Obedient, Polite, Responsible, Self-Controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy:** Values are arranged in a **priority hierarchy** — each individual ranks their terminal and instrumental values differently, creating a unique value system that drives behaviour.

---

### 2.5 Schwartz's Theory of Basic Human Values (Advanced)

Shalom H. Schwartz proposed **10 basic human values** organized along two bipolar dimensions:

| Dimension | Pole 1 | Pole 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Openness to Change** ↔ **Conservation** | Self-Direction, Stimulation, Hedonism | Security, Conformity, Tradition |
| **Self-Enhancement** ↔ **Self-Transcendence** | Power, Achievement | Universalism, Benevolence |

**Workplace relevance:** Values predict **organisational citizenship behaviour (OCB)**, **job satisfaction**, and **ethical decision-making**.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition | Key Theorist | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Personality | Dynamic organization of psychophysical systems | Allport | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Big Five (OCEAN) | Five broad trait dimensions | Costa & McCrae (1992) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| MBTI | 16-type classification from 4 dichotomies | Briggs & Myers (based on Jung) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Terminal Values | End-state goals | Rokeach | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Instrumental Values | Modes of conduct | Rokeach | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Person-Situation Debate | Behaviour = f(P, S) | Mischel | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Person-Job Fit | Match between individual traits and job demands | Muchinsky | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Person-Organization Fit | Match between individual values and org culture | Chatman | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

**Real-World Utility in Engineering Management:**
- **Recruitment & Selection:** HR departments use Big Five assessments to match candidates to job requirements.
- **Team Building:** Understanding diverse personality profiles helps in forming balanced project teams.
- **Leadership Development:** Self-awareness of personality helps emerging engineers in management roles.
- **Conflict Resolution:** Recognizing value differences between team members enables effective mediation.
- **Ethical Decision-Making:** Engineering ethics (e.g., whistle-blowing) often hinges on **terminal value conflicts** (e.g., "A Comfortable Life" vs "A World at Peace").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Frameworks, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Behaviour Equation — Exhaustive Derivation

In organizational behaviour, behaviour is not random. It is the outcome of a causal chain. KTU examinations often ask students to "explain the determinants of individual behaviour" — this framework is the standard answer.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= f(P_{\text{personality}}, V_{\text{values}}, M_{\text{motivation}}, E_{\text{environment}}) \\
B &= f\Big(\big(H, E_n, S\big), V, M, E\Big) \\
B &= f\big(P + V + M + E\big) \quad \text{(simplified interactionist model)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $B$ = Observable Behaviour
- $H$ = Heredity (genetic disposition)
- $E_n$ = Environment (nurture, culture, family)
- $S$ = Situation (immediate context)
- $V$ = Values (terminal + instrumental hierarchy)
- $M$ = Motivation (needs, drives, goals)
- $E$ = Environmental Forces (organisational culture, leadership style, group norms)

> **Derivation Logic (Textual Walkthrough):**
> - **Step 1:** Begin with the basic proposition that behaviour ($B$) is a function of internal dispositional factors.
> - **Step 2:** Expand the dispositional component into **Personality** (formed by heredity, environment, and situation) and **Values** (deepest belief structures).
> - **Step 3:** Add **Motivation** as a separate internal driver that activates latent traits.
> - **Step 4:** Layer in the **Environment** as the situational moderator.
> - **Step 5:** Recognize that the four components interact multiplicatively rather than additively, yielding the interactionist model $B = f(P, V, M, E)$ in its most parsimonious form.

---

### 3.2 Big Five Trait Sub-Facets (Expanded Analytical Matrix)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know the **sub-facets** (called "facets" in Costa & McCrae's NEO-PI-R framework). This matrix is high-yield for 14-mark questions.

| Trait | Sub-Facet | Description | Behavioural Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Openness** | Imagination | Vivid fantasy life | Daydreams frequently |
| | Artistic Interest | Appreciation of art & beauty | Visits museums, enjoys creative work |
| | Emotionality | Receptiveness to inner feelings | Aware of own emotions |
| | Adventurousness | Willingness to try new activities | Likes travel, novel experiences |
| | Intellect | Intellectual curiosity | Engages with abstract ideas |
| | Liberalism | Openness to new ideas, tolerance | Willing to question authority |
| **Conscientiousness** | Self-Efficacy | Belief in one's own competence | Confident in completing tasks |
| | Orderliness | Preference for neatness | Keeps organized workspace |
| | Dutifulness | Adherence to ethical principles | Reliable, follows rules |
| | Achievement-Striving | Drive for excellence | Sets high standards |
| | Self-Discipline | Ability to start and complete tasks | Finishes what is started |
| | Cautiousness | Tendency to think before acting | Avoids impulsive decisions |
| **Extraversion** | Friendliness | Genuine liking for others | Easily makes acquaintances |
| | Gregariousness | Preference for company of others | Enjoys parties, meetings |
| | Assertiveness | Social ascendancy, dominance | Speaks up in groups |
| | Activity Level | Pace of living | Fast-paced, energetic |
| | Excitement-Seeking | Need for thrills | Engages in high-risk activities |
| | Cheerfulness | Tendency to experience positive emotions | Generally optimistic |
| **Agreeableness** | Trust | Belief in others' sincerity | Assumes good faith |
| | Morality | Straightforwardness, frankness | Honest in dealings |
| | Altruism | Active concern for welfare of others | Volunteers, helps colleagues |
| | Cooperation | Disposition to work harmoniously | Compromises easily |
| | Modesty | Tendency to be unassuming | Does not seek attention |
| | Sympathy | Tender-mindedness, concern for others | Feels empathy for suffering |
| **Neuroticism** | Anxiety | Apprehensive, fearful | Worries frequently |
| | Anger | Tendency to experience anger | Gets irritated easily |
| | Depression | Tendency to feel sad, discouraged | Experiences mood drops |
| | Self-Consciousness | Sensitivity to criticism | Embarrasses easily |
| | Immoderation | Inability to control urges | Acts on whims |
| | Vulnerability | Susceptibility to stress | Easily overwhelmed |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Application: Person-Job Fit Analysis

This is a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU 2024 pattern. The framework below is the model answer structure.

**Step 1 — Identify Job Demands (Task Analysis)**
Enumerate the cognitive, emotional, and physical requirements of the job.
*Example:* A Customer Relationship Manager role demands empathy, communication, and emotional stability.

**Step 2 — Profile Personality Requirements**
Map demands onto Big Five traits.
*Example:*
- High Agreeableness → for empathy and cooperation
- High Extraversion → for client communication
- Low Neuroticism → for emotional stability under pressure

**Step 3 — Profile the Candidate**
Administer validated personality assessment (e.g., NEO-PI-R) and interpret scores.

**Step 4 — Compute Fit Index (Conceptual)**
The conceptual fit index is given by:

$$
\text{Fit Index} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot \vert P_i - J_i \vert}{5}
$$

where $P_i$ = candidate's score on trait $i$, $J_i$ = job's required score on trait $i$, and $w_i$ = relative weight of trait $i$ for the role. **A lower Fit Index indicates higher Person-Job Fit.**

> **Derivation Logic (Textual Walkthrough):**
> - **Step 1:** Convert raw trait scores onto a normalized scale (typically 0–100).
> - **Step 2:** Determine the job's "ideal" trait profile through job analysis.
> - **Step 3:** Calculate the absolute deviation $\vert P_i - J_i \vert$ for each of the five traits.
> - **Step 4:** Weight each deviation by the strategic importance of that trait to the job (e.g., Conscientiousness is critical for accountants but less so for creatives).
> - **Step 5:** Average the weighted deviations across all five traits to obtain a single composite Fit Index.
> - **Step 6:** Decision rule: Fit Index $< 15$ → Strong Fit; $15$–$30$ → Moderate Fit; $> 30$ → Misfit.

**Step 5 — Interpret and Decide**
Compare with the threshold, provide qualitative narrative, and integrate Person-Organization Fit (PO Fit) for the final selection.

---

### 3.4 Person-Organization (P-O) Fit Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Person-Organization Fit is the **compatibility between individuals and organizations** that occurs when their characteristics are well-matched. *(Chatman, 1989)*

The conceptual P-O fit equation is:

$$
\text{PO Fit} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j^{\text{individual}} \cdot v_j^{\text{organization}}}{n}
$$

where $v_j^{\text{individual}}$ is the individual's score on value $j$ and $v_j^{\text{organization}}$ is the organization's score on the same value $j$, with $n$ being the number of value dimensions considered. **Higher values indicate greater P-O fit.**

> [!TIP]
> **Workplace Application:** Companies like **Infosys, TCS, and Google** explicitly publish their value systems (e.g., "Customer-first," "Innovation," "Integrity") and screen for value congruence in interviews — this is the practical use of P-O fit analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Determinants of Personality (Interactionist Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PERSONALITY FORMATION"] --> B["HEREDITY"]
    A --> C["ENVIRONMENT"]
    A --> D["SITUATION"]

    B --> B1["Genes and Biological Factors"]
    B --> B2["Physical Appearance and Energy"]
    B --> B3["IQ and Temperament"]

    C --> C1["Culture"]
    C --> C2["Family and Socialization"]
    C --> C3["Education and Religion"]
    C --> C4["Social Class"]

    D --> D1["Immediate Context"]
    D --> D2["Social Setting"]
    D --> D3["Task Environment"]

    B1 --> OUT["RESULTING PERSONALITY TRAITS"]
    B2 --> OUT
    B3 --> OUT
    C1 --> OUT
    C2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
    C4 --> OUT
    D1 --> OUT
    D2 --> OUT
    D3 --> OUT

    OUT --> P1["OCEAN: Big Five Traits"]
    OUT --> P2["MBTI: 16 Types"]
    OUT --> P3["Values Hierarchy"]
    OUT --> P4["Self Concept"]

    P1 --> BEH["WORKPLACE BEHAVIOUR"]
    P2 --> BEH
    P3 --> BEH

```
